name: Red Rock, IA
description: Red Rock was a small farming community in Marion County, Iowa that thrived along the Des Moines River from the mid-1800s until the Army Corps of Engineers began construction of Red Rock Dam in the 1960s, permanently flooding the original townsite beneath Red Rock Lake. The town had a general store, church, and school before residents were relocated to higher ground. Today the original settlement lies beneath the reservoir, but during low-water periods the surrounding shoreline areas have yielded old foundations, ceramics, and personal items from the displaced community. The accessible shoreline makes this a worthwhile site for metal detectorists, particularly after seasonal drawdowns expose previously submerged ground.

name: Garrison, KS
description: Garrison was a small agricultural hamlet in Clay County, Kansas that developed alongside the railroad in the late nineteenth century as a shipping point for local grain and livestock. The town never grew large, and when highway routes bypassed the community in the early twentieth century, businesses relocated and residents drifted away. Little remains at the original site today beyond scattered foundations and overgrown lots, though the surrounding farmland retains its historic character. Detecting permission from local landowners can yield farm-era coins, buttons, and household hardware from the homesteading period.

name: Greenwich, MA
description: Greenwich was a prosperous agricultural village in Worcester County, Massachusetts that dated to colonial times and had a post office, churches, and a thriving mill economy along the Swift River valley. The town — along with the neighboring villages of Dana, Enfield, and Prescott — was condemned and flooded in the 1930s to create the Quabbin Reservoir, which now supplies drinking water to the Boston metropolitan area. Residents were forcibly relocated, and all structures were demolished before the valley was flooded by 1939. The surrounding Quabbin watershed is now protected state land; while metal detecting is prohibited within the reservation, low water periods occasionally expose the old roadbeds near the reservoir's edges.

name: Cannonsville, NY
description: Cannonsville was a rural Delaware County, New York farming community established in the early nineteenth century along the West Branch of the Delaware River, eventually growing to include a post office, churches, a store, and several hundred residents. In the 1950s the New York City Board of Water Supply condemned the town to build Cannonsville Reservoir as part of the Delaware River system, forcibly relocating families and demolishing all buildings before flooding the valley in 1964. Today the reservoir covers the original townsite completely, though during extended droughts the waterline drops to reveal old road surfaces and stone wall remnants. The surrounding watershed lands are managed by New York City DEP, restricting most recreational access.

name: Cebolla, CO
description: Cebolla was a small ranching and farming community in Gunnison County, Colorado situated along the Gunnison River in the Lake Fork valley, serving as a supply stop for homesteaders and cattlemen working the surrounding high country in the late 1800s. The town declined as improved roads and changing agricultural economics made the remote valley less viable for permanent settlement, and most residents had departed by the mid-twentieth century. A few old structures and foundations remain in the area, which is now surrounded by Gunnison National Forest and Bureau of Land Management land. The open public land makes access relatively straightforward, and old homesites in this region can yield late-Victorian era coins, buttons, and household items.

name: Iola, CO
description: Iola was a tiny settlement in Gunnison County, Colorado located in the Curecanti National Recreation Area along the Gunnison River, existing primarily as a small ranching and farming outpost in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. Like several nearby communities in the Lake Fork and Gunnison valleys, Iola was largely abandoned as the remote canyon country proved economically unviable for sustained settlement. Today the site falls within the boundaries of Curecanti National Recreation Area managed by the National Park Service, which prohibits metal detecting on federal park lands. The scenic canyon landscape preserves the historic setting even if the actual artifacts are off-limits to recreational collectors.

name: Torino, IL
description: Torino was a small agricultural village in Grundy County, Illinois that briefly flourished in the mid-nineteenth century as settlers moved into the Illinois prairie, establishing a post office, church, and handful of businesses to serve surrounding farms. When the county's railroad network bypassed Torino in favor of neighboring towns, commercial activity collapsed and the community dissolved over the following decades. Today the original townsite is incorporated into productive farmland with little surface evidence of its former existence. Detecting with landowner permission in former Illinois prairie towns like Torino can produce mid-1800s large cent and early American coinage alongside farm equipment remnants.

name: Kezar, CO
description: Kezar was a small agricultural settlement in Gunnison County, Colorado in the upper Gunnison River valley, serving as a modest supply point for surrounding ranches and farms in the late nineteenth century. Like many isolated Colorado high-country communities, Kezar faded as transportation improvements made it easier for valley residents to trade in larger centers, and the town was largely abandoned by the early twentieth century. The site today lies within or near Bureau of Land Management territory in the Gunnison Basin. Public land status makes some areas accessible for exploration, though the remote nature of the site requires significant travel to reach.

name: Arboles, CO
description: Arboles was a small ranching community in Archuleta County, Colorado near the confluence of the Piedra and San Juan rivers, serving as a local trading point for Hispanic and Anglo ranchers working the rugged high desert terrain in the late 1800s and early 1900s. The community declined as the regional economy shifted and Navajo Lake reservoir, created in the 1960s by Navajo Dam in neighboring New Mexico, altered the lower valley landscape. Some remnants of the old community survive in the area near Navajo State Park on the Colorado side of the lake. The state park and surrounding BLM lands offer relatively open access for history enthusiasts exploring the region.

name: Flagstaff, ME
description: Flagstaff was a small logging and farming community in Somerset County, Maine along the Dead River valley that existed from the colonial era until the 1950s, when Central Maine Power Company built Long Falls Dam and flooded the valley to create Flagstaff Lake. The town had a post office, church, and schoolhouse, and was notable as the last stopping point for Benedict Arnold's expedition to Quebec in 1775. Residents were bought out and displaced in the late 1940s before the dam began filling in 1950, and the structures were demolished or left to flood. During late summer and fall drawdowns, old foundations and cellar holes emerge along the lake's shoreline — a productive area for detecting with proper access permissions.

name: Bombay Beach Ruins, CA
description: Bombay Beach sits on the eastern shore of the Salton Sea in Imperial County, California, once a thriving resort community that boomed in the 1950s when the Salton Sea was marketed as a sparkling inland playground for boaters and vacationers. Rising salinity levels and agricultural runoff devastated fish populations and drove away tourists through the 1970s and 1980s, leaving most structures abandoned to the harsh desert climate. Today Bombay Beach is a surreal landscape of collapsed trailers, rusting car hulks, and half-submerged buildings partly reclaimed by the encroaching shoreline — one of Southern California's most photographed post-apocalyptic scenes. Access is open on public roads, but the soft shoreline mud and private property boundaries require careful navigation for artifact hunters.

name: Dickey, CO
description: Dickey was a small ranching and agricultural community in Summit County, Colorado situated in the upper Blue River valley, serving as a local supply point for surrounding homesteads and cattle operations in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The remote mountain location and harsh winters made year-round habitation difficult, and when highway improvements made it easier to reach the larger town of Breckenridge, Dickey gradually depopulated. Much of the land in the Blue River valley was later flooded by Dillon Reservoir when it was filled in 1963 to supply Denver's water needs. What wasn't submerged is now largely surrounded by White River National Forest, with the reservoir itself covering the original low-lying portions of the valley.

name: Millerton, CA
description: Millerton was a bustling Gold Rush–era town in Fresno County, California that developed along the San Joaquin River as a supply center and ferry crossing point during the mining and ranching boom of the 1850s, growing to include hotels, saloons, a courthouse, and several hundred residents. The original townsite was purchased by the Federal Bureau of Reclamation and flooded in 1947 when Friant Dam was completed, creating Millerton Lake. During severe drought years when water levels drop significantly, stone foundations, old roads, and even remnants of the original town's layout emerge from the lakebed — one of California's most remarkable submerged ghost towns. Millerton Lake State Recreation Area surrounds the reservoir, and while detecting on state park land requires a special permit, the shoreline history alone makes this a fascinating destination.

name: Republican City, NE (Old)
description: Republican City (Old) was the original settlement in Harlan County, Nebraska along the Republican River, platted in the 1870s as the county seat and a center of commerce for surrounding homesteaders during the great westward land rush of that era. The town was displaced when Harlan County Lake was created by the Army Corps of Engineers in the 1950s, inundating the original townsite beneath the reservoir waters. Today the lake is a popular recreation area, but during extended dry periods, old foundations and remnants of streets occasionally become visible along the shoreline. The surrounding Corps of Engineers managed land and state recreation area make this an accessible but regulated site for history enthusiasts.

name: Pactola, SD
description: Pactola was a community in Pennington County, South Dakota in the Black Hills along Rapid Creek, originally homesteaded in the 1870s during the Black Hills Gold Rush and sustained by a mill and local farming and ranching through the early twentieth century. The Bureau of Reclamation condemned the valley in the 1950s and completed Pactola Dam in 1956, flooding the town beneath Pactola Reservoir — the largest body of water in the Black Hills. A cemetery was relocated, and most structures were demolished before the valley was flooded. During significant drawdowns, traces of the old settlement have emerged near the reservoir's upper arms, and the surrounding Black Hills National Forest provides access to the historic landscape.

name: Dunreath, IA
description: Dunreath was a small Iowa prairie town in Wayne County that platted in the 1880s during the peak of Iowa's agricultural expansion, briefly supporting a post office, church, and a cluster of businesses serving surrounding farm families. The railroad bypassed Dunreath in favor of nearby communities, and the town never achieved the critical mass needed to sustain commerce in the competitive post-Civil War Iowa landscape. The site today is privately held farmland with few visible remnants of the original town. Detecting in former Iowa small towns with landowner permission commonly yields Seated Liberty and early Barber-era coinage, farm tokens, and nineteenth-century household items.

name: Fifield, IA
description: Fifield was a tiny nineteenth-century rural community in Davis County, Iowa that came and went within a generation of settlement, leaving little physical evidence of its brief existence on the rolling prairie landscape. Like dozens of would-be Iowa towns of the 1870s and 1880s, Fifield was platted speculatively and supported a post office for a short time before the population consolidated around more strategically located railroad towns. The original site is now productive farmland typical of central Iowa's rich agricultural landscape. Permission from the current landowners is needed to detect here, but the site could yield early Iowa settlement-era artifacts from the brief window of occupation.

name: Keetley, UT
description: Keetley was a small silver and lead mining camp in Summit County, Utah established in the 1890s in the Heber Valley, developing around the ore deposits of the nearby mines and serving as a residential and commercial hub for the surrounding mining district. The town supported a hotel, saloon, and several businesses before declining ore grades and shifting economics reduced operations through the early twentieth century. The site was later flooded when Jordanelle Reservoir was completed in 1993 by the Bureau of Reclamation, submerging much of the original community area. What remains above water is now accessible via the Jordanelle State Park, which surrounds the reservoir on the Wasatch Mountain slopes.

name: Jordanelle, UT
description: Jordanelle was a small ranching and farming community in Wasatch County, Utah in the Provo River valley, established by pioneer settlers in the late 1800s and sustained by cattle ranching and hay farming in the surrounding high-elevation meadows. The Bureau of Reclamation selected the valley for Jordanelle Dam, and construction completed in 1993 permanently flooded the old homestead sites beneath the reservoir. Several historic pioneer structures were documented before inundation, and the surrounding state park preserves interpretive information about the former community. The Jordanelle State Park offers public access to the reservoir shores, though metal detecting within state parks requires specific permits.

name: Hailstone, UT
description: Hailstone was a tiny ranch and farming outpost in Wasatch County, Utah near the confluence of Rock Creek and the Provo River, existing as a small service point for surrounding agricultural operations in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The community, like neighboring Jordanelle, was condemned and inundated when Jordanelle Reservoir filled in 1993. Interpretive displays at Jordanelle State Park reference the former settlements of the valley. Hailstone today is the name of the marina area on Jordanelle Reservoir's west arm — an ironic preservation of the old placename in a transformed landscape.

name: Monument City, IN
description: Monument City was a small Indiana farming community in Howard County that platted during the mid-nineteenth century land boom, briefly supporting a post office and local services before being absorbed by the growth of nearby Kokomo, which became the dominant commercial center of the county. The site today is subsumed within the greater Kokomo area, with no distinct physical remnant of the original plat. Farmstead-era artifact hunting in former Howard County communities can be productive when access is secured from private landowners.

name: Armstead, MT
description: Armstead was a small agricultural town in Beaverhead County, Montana along the Beaverhead River valley, established in the late nineteenth century as a ranching and farming hub for the surrounding high-elevation plateau. The town was condemned and flooded in the 1960s when the Bureau of Reclamation completed Clark Canyon Dam, creating Clark Canyon Reservoir to provide irrigation water for the Dillon area. Old foundations and remnants of the townsite sometimes emerge at the reservoir's upper end during low water years. The surrounding Bureau of Reclamation and BLM lands offer open access to history-minded visitors exploring this scenic southwest Montana landscape.

name: Melones, CA
description: Melones was a Gold Rush settlement in Calaveras County, California established in the 1850s along the Stanislaus River, growing through multiple mining eras to become a moderate-sized community with hotels, stores, and miners' amenities serving the hydraulic and dredge mining operations of the region. The original town was condemned and inundated in 1979 when New Melones Lake was created by the Army Corps of Engineers, submerging the historic townsite beneath hundreds of feet of water. During California's severe drought years, dramatically lowered reservoir levels occasionally expose portions of the old townsite including stone foundations and debris fields. The New Melones Lake area is managed by the Bureau of Reclamation and the state, with access varying by conditions.

name: Riverton, AL
description: Riverton was a small community in Cherokee County, Alabama that existed in the valley of the Coosa River before the construction of Weiss Lake by Alabama Power Company in 1961, which flooded many of the small river-bottom settlements of the region. The town had a modest agricultural and fishing economy tied to the natural rhythms of the Coosa River before impoundment transformed the landscape into one of Alabama's largest reservoirs. During drawdowns, stone foundations and old homesites have appeared along the lake's marshy margins. Weiss Lake is popular for recreation, and surrounding Cherokee County shoreline land is a mix of private and public access areas.

name: Easonville, AL
description: Easonville was one of several small communities in Talladega County, Alabama that was submerged beneath Lay Lake when Alabama Power Company dammed the Coosa River in 1914, making it one of the earliest Alabama River Power reservoirs and one of the state's first significant twentieth-century town-flooding events. The farming and timber community had sustained itself for decades along the fertile river bottomlands before the power company's land purchases and reservoir filling displaced residents. Fragments of the old community occasionally surface near the lake's shallower arms during drawdown periods. The Lay Lake area along the Coosa River corridor is popular for fishing and outdoor recreation.

name: Irma, AL
description: Irma was a small Alabama community in Talladega County displaced by the creation of Lay Lake when Alabama Power Company completed Logan Martin Dam's predecessor dam on the Coosa River in the early twentieth century, flooding bottomland farms and homesteads that had been established since the antebellum era. The community's residents were among the earliest Alabamians to experience the displacement that would become common with the extensive damming of Alabama's river system. The Coosa River lakes today form a popular recreation corridor, with some historic artifacts occasionally surfacing at the lower reservoir levels during dry-season drawdowns.

name: Ozella, AL
description: Ozella was an agricultural community in Talladega County, Alabama that existed along the Coosa River before the impoundment of Lay Lake by Alabama Power Company, farming the rich bottomlands that the river periodically flooded and enriched with silt. The town shared the fate of its neighbors Irma, Easonville, and others in the Coosa valley — condemned and flooded as the Alabama Power system of dams reshaped the river for hydroelectric production. Low-water periods at Lay Lake can expose old homesites near the lake's margins, particularly in the quieter coves where the original town areas once stood.

name: Susanna, AL
description: Susanna was a small farming community in Talladega County, Alabama along the Coosa River drainage, one of several rural settlements displaced by the construction of Alabama Power's hydroelectric dams that began transforming the Coosa River system in the early twentieth century. Like neighboring communities Ozella and Irma, Susanna represented the small-scale agricultural life of Alabama's piedmont river valleys that was permanently altered by industrialization and electrification. The Lay Lake area today offers recreation access, and during extreme low-water periods some of the older shoreline areas reveal structural remnants from the pre-reservoir era.

name: Prairie Bluff, AL
description: Prairie Bluff was an antebellum Alabama community in Wilcox County along the Alabama River, once a significant cotton shipping point where steamboats loaded bales for the journey down to Mobile Bay and the broader cotton trade network of the Deep South. The town thrived in the 1840s and 1850s on King Cotton's prosperity but declined sharply after the Civil War as the plantation economy collapsed and the Alabama River's commercial role diminished with the coming of railroads. Today only scattered remnants mark the site, which is on private land typical of Alabama's Black Belt rural landscape. The antebellum history makes this an intriguing research destination, though access requires landowner permission.

name: New Burlington, Clinton County, OH
description: New Burlington was a Quaker farming community in Clinton County, Ohio established in the early nineteenth century by Friends who had migrated from North Carolina and Virginia seeking fertile Ohio farmland and freedom from the slave economy of the South. The community thrived for generations, with a meetinghouse and school serving as the center of civic life for surrounding Quaker farms. New Burlington was condemned in the 1960s to construct Caesar Creek Lake by the Army Corps of Engineers, flooding the historic Quaker town beneath the reservoir waters. The Caesar Creek State Park surrounds the lake and offers access to the general area, with the former town now interpreted at the park's visitor center.

name: Livermore, PA
description: Livermore was a small community in Westmoreland County, Pennsylvania along the Conemaugh River that dated to the early nineteenth century, developing around a river crossing and mill site that served travelers and local farmers in the southwestern Pennsylvania hills. The town's fate was sealed when the Army Corps of Engineers built Conemaugh River Lake — a flood control reservoir completed in 1952 — inundating the Conemaugh valley to protect Pittsburgh and downstream communities from floods like the catastrophic Johnstown Flood of 1889. The reservoir operates in dry mode, holding back water only during flood events, which means the old townsite is occasionally exposed during normal conditions — a fascinating window into a submerged western Pennsylvania community. The surrounding Westmoreland County landscape is privately owned, requiring permission to access the former townsite.

name: Tartown, PA
description: Tartown was a small community in Westmoreland County, Pennsylvania that also fell within the Conemaugh River Lake flood control pool, sharing the fate of neighboring Livermore when the Army Corps of Engineers acquired and cleared the Conemaugh River valley for the dry dam project completed in 1952. The community had existed as a modest farming and mill settlement for over a century before being condemned. Since the dam operates primarily empty except during flood events, the former townsite is accessible much of the year, and old building foundations, cellar holes, and remnant orchards are sometimes visible. The flood pool lands are managed by the Army Corps and access policies vary by location.

name: Dana, MA
description: Dana was one of four Swift River valley towns in Worcester County, Massachusetts — along with Greenwich, Enfield, and Prescott — condemned and demolished in the 1930s so the Metropolitan District Commission could flood the valley and create the Quabbin Reservoir to supply Boston's drinking water. The town had thrived since colonial times, with farms, mills, and a classic New England village character centered on a Congregational church and town common. More than 2,500 people were displaced as their homes, churches, and cemeteries were razed or relocated by 1939. The Quabbin watershed is protected state land where metal detecting is not permitted, but the interpretive facilities and occasional archaeology programs make this a historically rich destination.

name: Enfield, MA
description: Enfield was another of the four Swift River valley towns erased by the Quabbin Reservoir project in central Massachusetts, a prosperous farming community with a long Congregational history dating to the 1700s that was completely demolished and flooded by 1939. The Swift River valley towns collectively represented an entire subregion of rural Massachusetts that was sacrificed to provide drinking water for the growing Boston metropolitan population — a contentious land-taking that remains a point of regional memory. The Quabbin Reservoir watershed is now strictly protected state land administered by the Massachusetts Department of Conservation and Recreation, and detecting or digging is prohibited throughout the reservation. Annual Quabbin Commemorations draw descendants of the displaced families to remember the lost communities.

name: Evarts, SD
description: Evarts was a small South Dakota farming community in Charles Mix County that platted during the homesteading era of the late nineteenth century, supporting a post office and local agricultural commerce before the marginal soil conditions and harsh climate of the Missouri River Breaks made sustainable farming difficult. The community dissolved in the early twentieth century as drought and economic pressures consolidated the population into larger regional centers. The former townsite is now privately held grassland in the Missouri River agricultural region of southeast South Dakota. Homestead-era artifacts from short-lived Dakota Territory communities occasionally surface at such sites with landowner permission.

name: Crouse, NE
description: Crouse was a small Nebraska farming community in Antelope County that briefly held a post office in the late nineteenth century before being absorbed into the surrounding agricultural landscape as the county's settlement patterns consolidated around larger railroad towns. The site is now typical north-central Nebraska farmland with few visible remnants of the original platted community. Detecting with permission in former Nebraska post office towns can yield copper and silver coinage from the territorial and early statehood era.

name: American Falls original townsite
description: American Falls (original townsite) was the original settlement in Power County, Idaho along the Snake River that dated to the fur trade era and grew into a modest agricultural and ranching community serving the upper Snake River valley in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The original town was condemned and inundated in 1927 when American Falls Dam was completed, raising the Snake River's level and flooding the original site beneath American Falls Reservoir. The town was relocated to higher ground, and the new American Falls still thrives today, but the original townsite lies beneath the reservoir. During severe drought years, portions of the original foundation remnants have been observed during drawdown periods.

name: Osborn, OH
description: Osborn was a small Ohio farming community in Greene County that existed in the Mad River valley before the Army Corps of Engineers constructed Huffman Dam in the 1940s, converting the valley into a dry flood-control reservoir known as Huffman Lake. The community, along with neighboring Fairfield, was acquired and cleared by the Corps to protect Dayton from catastrophic flooding following the devastating 1913 Flood that killed hundreds in the Miami Valley. The dry reservoir area is now part of Wright-Patterson Air Force Base's environmental reservation, making public access highly restricted. The military nature of the surrounding land closes this area to recreational metal detecting.

name: Alluwe, OK
description: Alluwe was a Cherokee Nation community in Craig County, Oklahoma that was established in Indian Territory following the removal era, developing as a small farming and ranching settlement along the Verdigris River with a post office, school, and trading post. The town was condemned and flooded in the 1960s when the Army Corps of Engineers completed Pensacola Dam and created Grand Lake O' the Cherokees, one of Oklahoma's largest reservoirs. The history of Alluwe is entwined with the complex story of the Five Civilized Tribes who rebuilt their nations in Indian Territory only to face further displacement from resource projects in the twentieth century. The Grand Lake area in northeast Oklahoma is popular for recreation, with some shoreline access available around the reservoir.

name: Kaskaskia, IL
description: Kaskaskia was the original capital of Illinois and one of the oldest European settlements in the American Midwest, a French colonial town established in the early 1700s along the Mississippi River that later became the state's first capital in 1818. The town was devastated by repeated Mississippi River floods and channel migration — the most catastrophic in 1881, when the river cut a new channel and literally moved the town to the Missouri side of its historical boundary with Illinois, creating an anomalous piece of Illinois territory accessible only through Missouri. The historic Kaskaskia Island retains its Illinois jurisdiction and the historic Kaskaskia Bell, but only a handful of residents remain. Metal detecting on Kaskaskia Island is subject to state and private landowner rules, but the colonial history of the site makes it remarkable for potential artifact recovery.

name: San Marcial, NM
description: San Marcial was a New Mexico town in Socorro County along the Rio Grande that endured multiple flood disasters — in 1895, 1929, and most catastrophically in 1929 — before being completely abandoned after a flood destroyed most of the town and the railroad relocated its facilities. The town had originally served as a railroad supply and division point for the Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe Railway, growing to include a hotel, multiple stores, and a resident population of over a thousand people at its peak. Today the ruins of San Marcial lie within the Bosque del Apache National Wildlife Refuge, where they are protected and largely off-limits to artifact recovery. The adobe ruins visible from refuge roads are a striking reminder of the Rio Grande's historic tendency to reclaim its floodplain.

name: Gilman, CO
description: Gilman was a zinc and silver mining company town perched dramatically on a cliff above the Eagle River canyon in Eagle County, Colorado, operated almost entirely by the Eagle Mine from the early 1900s through 1984, when a Superfund designation forced closure after decades of mining left the site severely contaminated with heavy metals. At its peak Gilman housed several hundred miners and their families in company-owned housing, with a school, store, and community facilities all managed by the mining company. Today the entire site is fenced off and remediated under EPA oversight — the cliffside cluster of abandoned buildings is clearly visible from US Highway 24 below, but the site is strictly off-limits to the public. Metal detecting and entry are prohibited due to contamination hazards, making Gilman one of Colorado's most visually striking but completely inaccessible ghost towns.

name: Millville, IL
description: Millville was a small farming community in Pike County, Illinois along the Illinois River that thrived in the mid-nineteenth century as a river trade and agricultural center, supporting mills and businesses that served the surrounding river valley farmsteads. The community declined as navigation on the Illinois River became less economically central and larger nearby towns captured the regional commercial trade. The site today is low-lying agricultural land typical of western Illinois's river bottom terrain. Pike County farmstead sites from the antebellum period can yield early American coinage and household artifacts when detecting permission is obtained from current landowners.

name: Kapoho, HI
description: Kapoho was a small farming community on the eastern tip of the Big Island of Hawaii in Puna District that was first devastated by lava flows from Kilauea in 1960, destroying much of the original village, and then completely obliterated in May 2018 when a catastrophic eruption sent lava rivers across the lower Puna landscape, burying what had been rebuilt under tens of feet of new land. The original community grew warm-water orchids, papayas, and anthurium flowers in the fertile volcanic soil, and Kapoho Bay — a beloved snorkeling destination — was completely filled by the 2018 flows. Today the site is new lava rock covering former homes, businesses, and the bay itself, dramatically demonstrating the ongoing geological youth of Hawaii's Big Island. The fresh lava fields and private land status mean the site is largely inaccessible for any kind of artifact recovery.

name: Atomic City, ID
description: Atomic City is a small Idaho community in Bingham County southeast of Idaho Falls that boomed briefly in the 1950s as a bedroom community for workers at the National Reactor Testing Station (now Idaho National Laboratory), where the United States conducted pioneering nuclear reactor experiments during the Cold War. The town was named with typical 1950s atomic-age optimism and reached a population of several hundred at its peak during the height of nuclear research activity at the nearby facility. As federal employment at INL has fluctuated and the nuclear program evolved, Atomic City has shrunk to barely a dozen residents — technically still incorporated but effectively a ghost of its Cold War self. The surrounding INL reservation is federal land with restricted access, though Atomic City's surviving structures and the area's Cold War history make it a fascinating roadside stop.

name: Centralia, PA
description: Centralia was a thriving anthracite coal town in Columbia County, Pennsylvania until 1962, when a trash fire in an abandoned strip mine ignited an underground coal seam that has been burning ever since, slowly undermining the town with toxic gases and ground subsidence. Over the following decades, carbon monoxide and sulfur dioxide seeping from the ground forced the evacuation of nearly all of the town's 1,100 residents, and most structures were demolished by the state. Today only a handful of buildings remain on a cracked, steam-venting grid of streets — Pennsylvania's most famous ghost town and one of the eeriest abandoned places in America. The former residential blocks and demolished building lots are accessible from the public roads, though underground instability makes the ground itself potentially hazardous.

name: Isles Dernieres, LA
description: Isles Dernieres — or Last Island — was a popular antebellum resort island off the Louisiana coast in Terrebonne Parish that drew wealthy New Orleans society families to its hotels and boardinghouses during the steaming summer months of the 1840s and 1850s. The island was catastrophically destroyed on August 10, 1856, when a powerful hurricane made direct landfall, killing over 200 of the approximately 400 people present — one of the deadliest disasters in Louisiana history before Katrina. The remnant island chain called Isle Dernieres continues to shrink through ongoing coastal erosion and land subsidence, and today barely stands above sea level. The site is in the Gulf of Mexico and largely inaccessible except by boat, with ongoing erosion continuing to diminish what little land remains.

name: Kalapana, HI
description: Kalapana was a residential community on Hawaii's Big Island in the Puna District that was built on old lava flows from previous volcanic activity, growing in the mid-twentieth century as a residential area attracting people drawn to the remote, affordable Big Island countryside. In 1990 a lava flow from Kilauea's ongoing eruption crept inexorably through the neighborhood, burying over 100 homes, the famous Kaimū Black Sand Beach, and most of the community's infrastructure under fresh volcanic rock. The Royal Gardens subdivision above Kalapana continued to be eaten by lava flows through subsequent years, and the 2018 eruption delivered the final blow to any remaining structures. The lava-covered former streets are accessible on foot, a surreal landscape where old asphalt roads disappear beneath new rock.

name: Thistle, UT
description: Thistle was a small Utah County, Utah community along the Spanish Fork River that was obliterated in the spring of 1983 when an enormous landslide blocked the canyon, creating a natural dam that rapidly backed up a lake that submerged the town, the railroad, and U.S. Highway 6 under several dozen feet of water in one of the costliest landslide disasters in American history. The Army Corps of Engineers drilled a drainage tunnel through the slide mass to drain the lake, costing over $400 million in 1983 dollars — the most expensive landslide mitigation project ever undertaken in the United States. The town was never rebuilt, and the canyon today shows the dramatic scar of the slide with new highway and railroad alignments routed around the obstruction. The drained lakebed still shows evidence of the former townsite, accessible via the canyon road during favorable conditions.

name: Kirwin, WY
description: Kirwin was a small ranching and farming community in Phillips County, Wyoming — actually Philips County, Kansas, just north of the Wyoming border — that developed in the 1870s along the North Fork of the Solomon River, growing to include a post office, hotel, grain elevator, and several businesses before drought and the collapse of the cattle market in the late 1880s devastated the economy. The community recovered partially in the early twentieth century but ultimately declined as population drained from northwest Kansas's marginal agricultural country. Today Kirwin is the site of Kirwin National Wildlife Refuge, established around Kirwin Reservoir, which covers much of the original town area. The NWR land makes metal detecting prohibited, though the wildlife refuge preserves some of the area's natural and historic character.

name: Appleton, Illinois
description: Appleton was a small farming community in Hancock County, Illinois along the Illinois River that developed in the mid-nineteenth century as a river landing and agricultural supply point, serving the surrounding flat prairie farmland during the great Illinois settlement era. The town struggled to maintain commercial viability as larger river towns monopolized trade and rail connections bypassed many smaller Illinois River communities. The site today is low-lying Illinois bottomland, privately farmed with few above-ground remnants of the original community. River-bottom Illinois sites from the 1840s-1880s can yield interesting finds including boat hardware, trade goods, and agricultural implements when access is secured.

name: Brownsville, Jackson County, IL
description: Brownsville was a small rural community in Jackson County, Illinois in the Shawnee Hills region of southern Illinois, a part of the state settled early in the nineteenth century by Upland South migrants who established small farms and local trade centers in the wooded hills. The community faded as transportation routes evolved and the relative isolation of the Shawnee region made it difficult to sustain commercial growth. Today the former site is within or near the Shawnee National Forest, which covers much of Jackson County's public lands. Detecting in the Shawnee region requires careful attention to land status, as public forest land prohibits artifact removal while private agricultural parcels may be accessed with permission.

name: Pinhook, MO
description: Pinhook was a small Missouri bootheel community in Mississippi County that was entirely destroyed when the Birds Point-New Madrid Floodway was intentionally detonated by the Army Corps of Engineers in May 2011, flooding the community to relieve pressure on the levee system during the catastrophic Mississippi River flood of that year. The predominantly African American community of Pinhook had 26 homes, a church, and a community center that were all destroyed when the levee was blown — and unlike the hundreds of thousands of acres of farmland that the flood control measure was designed to protect, Pinhook's residents were not compensated fairly and could not rebuild. The site today is private farmland in the Missouri bootheel, with the community's story serving as a stark example of environmental injustice. The former community area is now low-lying cropland with little surface evidence of the homes and lives lost.

name: Byrnesville, Pennsylvania
description: Byrnesville was a small Pennsylvania coal mining community in Carbon County that developed to house miners working the region's extensive anthracite deposits, building the close-knit working-class social structure typical of Pennsylvania coal patch towns with a church, company store, and rows of company-owned housing. Like dozens of other carbon county coal patches, Byrnesville declined as deep mining operations closed and miners sought work in other industries or relocated to larger centers. The site today shows typical post-coal patch characteristics — some foundation remnants, disturbed ground, and the coal dust that still lingers in the soil of former mining communities. Metal detecting in former Pennsylvania coal patches can yield late-Victorian coins, mining company tokens, and immigrant-era personal artifacts.

name: Ben Ficklin, TX
description: Ben Ficklin was the original county seat of Tom Green County, Texas located at the confluence of Spring Creek and the Middle Concho River, established in 1875 and serving as a significant supply point along the frontier military road system in the post-Civil War development of West Texas. The town was catastrophically destroyed on August 23, 1882, when a flash flood down the Concho swept through the settlement, killing over 65 people in one of Texas's deadliest natural disasters and washing away most of the town's structures. The county seat was moved to the surviving higher ground at nearby San Angelo, and Ben Ficklin was never rebuilt. The site today is private ranchland outside San Angelo, and the historic marker commemorating the flood can be found near the original location.

name: Texon, TX
description: Texon was a West Texas company oil town in Reagan County that emerged from the desert literally overnight after the discovery of the prolific Santa Rita No. 1 oil well in 1923, with the Texon Oil and Land Company building an entire planned community including housing, a hotel, school, country club, and movie theater to support the exploding oil field workforce. The company town thrived through the boom years of West Texas oil production but began declining in the 1950s as oil production gradually waned and the Texon company restructured. The company sold off properties and Texon was depopulated by the 1960s, leaving mostly empty lots and a handful of surviving structures baking in the West Texas sun. The remote site in the Permian Basin is accessible by road, and period oil field and domestic artifacts have been found in the area.

name: La Balize, LA
description: La Balize was a French colonial river pilot station and small community at the very mouth of the Mississippi River in Plaquemines Parish, Louisiana, established by the French in the early 1700s as a mandatory stopping point where incoming ships took on river pilots familiar with the treacherous delta passes. The settlement at the Southwest Pass entrance served for generations as America's gateway to the inland continent via New Orleans, though the low-lying delta land was perpetually at risk from storms and flood. Over centuries of storms, floods, and Mississippi River channel migration, the original settlement sites have been repeatedly destroyed and rebuilt until the land itself was lost to Gulf of Mexico erosion. Today the original La Balize site is under Gulf of Mexico waters — one of Louisiana's most historically significant lost places.

name: Mayville, IL
description: Mayville was a small agricultural community in Madison County, Illinois in the American Bottom region near the confluence of the Missouri and Mississippi Rivers, developing in the early nineteenth century as a farming supply point in this fertile but flood-prone lowland landscape. The Mississippi River's periodic destructive floods and the availability of better-situated town sites gradually drained Mayville of its population and commerce. The site is now within the broader St. Louis metropolitan area's Madison County bottomland, with the original community long since absorbed into the landscape. The American Bottom region has yielded French colonial and early American artifacts at sites where the original ground surface survives beneath agricultural disturbance.

name: Beryl
description: Beryl was a small junction community in Iron County, Utah along the route of the now-defunct Union Pacific branch line that served the agriculture and mining interests of the Escalante Valley in the early twentieth century. The town existed primarily as a railroad service point and small farming community before rail service declined and the branch line was eventually abandoned, leaving the community without its primary economic reason for existence. The site today preserves a small remnant settlement, with the surrounding agricultural land of the Escalante Valley still actively farmed. Former railroad junction communities in rural Utah can yield railroad hardware, early automotive era items, and domestic artifacts from the 1910s-1950s with landowner permission.

name: Valmeyer, IL (Original location)
description: Valmeyer (original location) was a small village in Monroe County, Illinois along the Mississippi River floodplain that was catastrophically flooded in the Great Flood of 1993 — one of the most damaging floods in American history — which put the entire town underwater for months and destroyed or severely damaged most structures. Rather than rebuild in the floodplain, residents voted to relocate the entire community to the bluffs above the river, moving Valmeyer to higher ground and creating a rare modern example of deliberate whole-community relocation in American history. The original townsite has been largely cleared and returned to floodplain, with only scattered foundation remnants and the altered landscape speaking to the community that stood there for over a century. The relocation story makes Valmeyer a fascinating modern ghost town case study.

name: Weston, IL
description: Weston was a small Iroquois County, Illinois community in the flat agricultural heartland of northeastern Illinois that existed briefly as a post office and farming service point during the mid-to-late nineteenth century before being eclipsed by neighboring railroad communities that had superior transportation connections. The Illinois prairie landscape around Weston has been intensively farmed for over 150 years, and little surface evidence of the original platted community remains today. Former post office communities in Iroquois County can yield early Illinois settlement artifacts with landowner permission, including early American coinage and homestead hardware from the 1860s-1880s.

name: Vishnu Springs, IL
description: Vishnu Springs was a short-lived Illinois resort destination in Pike County developed in the 1890s around natural mineral springs that were marketed as having medicinal properties — a common commercial formula of the Gilded Age when mineral spring resorts proliferated across America. The resort included a hotel, bathhouses, and mineral spring facilities that attracted health-seeking visitors from the St. Louis and western Illinois region during its brief heyday. The resort fell out of fashion as mineral spring therapy lost its popular appeal in the early twentieth century, and the property eventually returned to agricultural use. The site is on private land in western Illinois, and old resort grounds in this region can occasionally yield period glassware, ceramic fragments, and personal items from the resort era.

name: Nothing, AZ
description: Nothing was a roadside community in Yavapai County, Arizona along State Route 93 between Phoenix and Las Vegas that consisted of a gas station, small store, and handful of buildings serving the sparse desert highway traffic through the Sonoran Desert's Big Sandy Valley. The community existed at the margins of viability for decades, with its population ranging from single digits to just a few dozen at most, relying entirely on passing motorists for survival. The last business closed in the early 2000s, leaving only the roadside signs and some concrete foundations in the creosote-covered Sonoran Desert landscape. The site is accessible from the highway, and the surrounding desert terrain offers open BLM land for exploration.

name: Ludlow, CA
description: Ludlow was a California desert town in San Bernardino County along the historic National Old Trails Road (later Route 66) that served as a water and fuel stop for the Santa Fe Railway and early automobile travelers crossing the Mojave Desert in the early twentieth century. The town had a hotel, cafe, gas stations, and several businesses catering to both railroad workers and highway travelers during the Route 66 golden age of the 1940s-1960s. Interstate 40 bypassed Ludlow in 1972, stranding it off the main highway and triggering a long decline — today only a few businesses cling to life on the interstate on-ramp while the original town buildings slowly deteriorate in the desert. The accessible ruins and surrounding Mojave Desert BLM land make this a productive area for Route 66–era artifact hunting.

name: Tuscumbia, IL
description: Tuscumbia was a small farming community in Schuyler County, Illinois that developed in the mid-nineteenth century as an agricultural service point for the surrounding prairie farmland, supporting a post office and local businesses for a few decades before population consolidated in the county's railroad towns. The central Illinois landscape around Tuscumbia has been continuously farmed since the 1830s, with the original village site now private agricultural land. Schuyler County farmstead and village sites from the antebellum period can be productive for early American coinage, Civil War-era personal items, and nineteenth-century domestic artifacts.

name: Tedens, IL
description: Tedens was a tiny rural community in Iroquois County, Illinois that briefly held a post office in the late nineteenth century while serving the surrounding flat prairie farmsteads in this intensively agricultural northeastern Illinois county. Like many small Illinois post office communities, Tedens never developed the railroad connection needed to sustain permanent commercial growth, and the community dissolved as its residents traded in larger nearby centers. The site is now active farmland in one of Illinois's most productive agricultural counties. Former rural Illinois post office communities rarely have surviving above-ground features but can yield homestead-era coins and artifacts in surrounding fields with landowner permission.

name: Sanish, ND
description: Sanish was a prosperous agricultural community in Mountrail County, North Dakota in the Missouri River valley that thrived from the early twentieth century through the 1950s as a farming and ranching center serving the Fort Berthold Indian Reservation area. The town was condemned and flooded when Garrison Dam was completed in 1953, creating Lake Sakakawea — the third-largest man-made reservoir in the United States — which permanently inundated Sanish along with its neighbors Van Hook and Elbowoods. The flooding of Sanish represented a profound loss for the Three Affiliated Tribes (Mandan, Hidatsa, and Arikara) who lived in the valley, displacing over 900 families from their ancestral homeland. During significant drawdowns of Lake Sakakawea, portions of the old townsite have been observed near the reservoir's upper arms.

name: Seewhy, MI
description: Seewhy was a tiny northern Michigan community in Antrim County that existed briefly in the late nineteenth century during the lumbering era that stripped the forests of northern Michigan at an industrial pace. The small settlement served as a logging camp support point in what was then densely forested hardwood and pine country before the timber was exhausted and the lumbermen moved on. The northern Michigan cut-over lands were then attempted as farmland — largely unsuccessfully — and much of the territory eventually reverted to second-growth forest. The Antrim County landscape today is mixed second-growth forest and agricultural land, with old logging era sites sometimes yielding camp equipment and personal items from the 1870s-1900s.

name: Sartoria, NE
description: Sartoria was a small farming community in Antelope County, Nebraska that platted in the homesteading era of the 1870s-1880s during the great rush to settle the Great Plains, supporting a post office and modest commercial services for surrounding agricultural families. The community failed to develop the railroad connection necessary to sustain long-term growth, and as neighboring towns with better transportation links absorbed the regional trade, Sartoria declined and its post office closed. The site today is typical north-central Nebraska agricultural land, privately held and actively farmed. Former Nebraska homestead-era communities in Antelope County can yield Victorian-era coins and farm implements when detection permission is granted by landowners.

name: Millsdale, IL
description: Millsdale was a small farming community in Grundy County, Illinois that developed in the mid-nineteenth century along a tributary of the Illinois River as a mill site and agricultural support community, briefly serving the surrounding farmsteads before larger, better-situated communities captured the regional trade. The Illinois and Michigan Canal corridor in Grundy County was intensively settled in the 1830s-1850s, and many small communities like Millsdale that failed to secure railroad connections withered within a generation. The site today is agricultural land in Grundy County's flat prairie landscape. The canal era and subsequent agricultural settlement of this region makes Grundy County a historically rich area for detecting when private land access can be arranged.

name: LeBeau, SD
description: LeBeau was the original county seat of Campbell County, South Dakota, a Missouri River community that thrived as an agricultural trading center and government seat in the late nineteenth century before the Missouri River repeatedly threatened and ultimately undermined the town's position. The town was inundated by Lake Oahe when Oahe Dam was completed in 1962 by the Army Corps of Engineers, creating one of the world's largest reservoirs by surface area and displacing communities throughout the Standing Rock Sioux and Cheyenne River Sioux territories. During extended droughts and seasonal drawdowns, portions of the LeBeau townsite have been exposed, revealing old streets and building foundations. The surrounding Lake Oahe reservoir area spans South and North Dakota with variable public access along its vast shoreline.

name: Columbiana, IL
description: Columbiana was a small farming community in Randolph County, Illinois in the southeastern Illinois hill country that developed in the first half of the nineteenth century as a local trading and mill center for the surrounding small farms. The rugged terrain of Randolph and Randolph County's hill country preserved smaller, less commercially connected communities like Columbiana longer than the flat prairie regions, but the community ultimately declined as the regional economy consolidated. The site is now privately held rural land in southern Illinois. Randolph County's long settlement history, dating to the French colonial period along the Mississippi, makes southern Illinois a historically rich detecting region when access can be arranged.

name: Boyle, IL
description: Boyle was a tiny agricultural community in Randolph County, Illinois that existed briefly as a local service point for the surrounding farming area in the nineteenth century, supporting a post office for a short time before the community dissolved as population concentrated in larger market towns. The site today is private farmland in the rolling terrain of southern Illinois's Randolph County. This region of Illinois, settled from the early American colonial period, can yield a variety of nineteenth-century artifacts in former community sites.

name: Reardon, IL
description: Reardon was a small community in Madison County, Illinois in the American Bottom region near the confluence of the Missouri and Mississippi Rivers, one of the most historically significant geographic zones in North American history as the crossroads of French, Native American, and American frontier cultures. The community developed in the farming era following American settlement of the region but failed to sustain growth as larger American Bottom towns captured the regional commerce. The low-lying American Bottom landscape around Reardon has been subject to repeated Mississippi River floods that complicate both settlement and preservation. The region's long and rich history from the French colonial era through the American frontier period makes it archaeologically significant.

name: Choate, MI
description: Choate was a small Michigan community in Mackinac County in the Upper Peninsula that developed during the lumbering era of the late nineteenth century when northern Michigan's forests were systematically clear-cut by industrial logging operations. Like dozens of Upper Peninsula lumber towns, Choate existed to support logging camp operations and faded rapidly once the timber was exhausted in the early twentieth century. The cut-over lands of Mackinac County were largely unsuccessful as farmland and have reverted to second-growth forest, much of it now in national forest or state-owned land. Old logging camp sites in the Upper Peninsula can yield period equipment, camp hardware, and personal items from the 1880s-1910s in areas where land access is permitted.

name: Three Forks of Gauley, WV
description: Three Forks of Gauley was a small community at the junction of three tributaries of the Gauley River in Nicholas County, West Virginia, a remote mountain settlement that served the limited farming and timbering activities of the rugged Appalachian terrain in the mid-nineteenth century. The community never grew large given the challenging mountain landscape, and improved transportation elsewhere made the isolated location untenable for sustained commercial activity. The site today is within the Monongahela National Forest or surrounding private mountain land in one of West Virginia's most remote and scenic regions. The rugged terrain limits access but the forest and river corridor make for spectacular countryside.

name: Farnham, CO
description: Farnham was a small farming community in Prowers County, Colorado in the southeastern Colorado plains along the Arkansas River drainage that developed during the homesteading era of the late nineteenth century as settlers moved into the semiarid High Plains. Periodic drought, the collapse of the cattle market in 1886-1887, and the marginal nature of dryland farming in southeast Colorado combined to defeat most small communities like Farnham that lacked railroad connections. The site today is typical southeastern Colorado high plains farmland and ranchland, privately held. Former homestead communities in the Arkansas River valley region can yield late-Victorian domestic artifacts and farm implements with landowner permission.

name: Hancock, CO
description: Hancock was a small railroad supply community at the summit of the Continental Divide in Chaffee County, Colorado along the Denver, South Park & Pacific Railroad's Alpine Tunnel route, established in the 1880s as a supply and maintenance base for the railroad's challenging high-altitude operations through the 11,523-foot Alpine Tunnel. The railroad ceased operations through the tunnel in 1910 due to the prohibitive cost of maintaining the high-altitude route, and the railroad facilities and support community at Hancock were abandoned. The original site at roughly 11,000 feet in elevation in the Sawatch Range is accessible via a rough 4WD road and sits within San Isabel National Forest. Several log building remnants survive at the site, and the surrounding mine dumps and railroad grade are rich territory for period artifacts.

name: Van Cleve, IA
description: Van Cleve was a small agricultural community in Story County, Iowa that platted during the peak of Iowa's settlement era in the 1870s-1880s, briefly supporting a post office and local commercial services for surrounding farm families. The community never secured a railroad connection and was quickly outcompeted by railroad towns in Story County, which is home to Iowa State University and the prosperous city of Ames. The site today is private farmland in one of Iowa's most productive agricultural counties. Story County's long farming history from the 1850s onward makes former post office communities like Van Cleve productive sites for detecting with landowner permission.

name: Crush, TX
description: Crush was a small Texas community in McLennan County that achieved its sole moment of fame on September 15, 1896, when William George Crush of the Missouri-Kansas-Texas Railroad staged a deliberate head-on collision of two locomotives as a publicity stunt, attracting an estimated 40,000 spectators — the largest gathering in Texas history to that date. The collision was catastrophically more violent than anticipated, with the boilers exploding and killing two spectators and injuring many more; Crush was fired the same day but rehired the next. The site near West, Texas (McLennan County) was never a permanent community and returned to farmland immediately after the event. The historical significance of the site is documented only by markers, as no community infrastructure was ever built there.

name: Mangum, TX
description: Mangum was a small West Texas ranching community in Stonewall County in the rolling red clay hills of the Rolling Plains region, one of many short-lived settlements that attempted to establish permanent communities on the marginal ranchland of west-central Texas in the late nineteenth century. Without a railroad connection and subject to the boom-bust cycles of Texas cattle ranching, Mangum faded quickly as the region's livestock economy became increasingly centralized in larger market towns with rail access. The site today is private ranchland typical of Stonewall County's working cattle country. The red clay soil of the Rolling Plains is hard on artifacts but former ranch communities can yield cowboy-era hardware, ranch equipment fragments, and occasional coinage.

name: Sipe Springs, TX
description: Sipe Springs was a small Comanche County, Texas community in the Cross Timbers region that developed around natural springs in the late nineteenth century as a ranching and farming supply point in the rough post oak and mesquite terrain of central Texas. The springs provided reliable water in a region where that commodity was precious, and the community briefly supported a hotel, school, and businesses. Improved wells and alternative water sources, combined with the lack of railroad access, ultimately undermined the community's spring-based advantage. The site today is private ranchland in Comanche County, and the surrounding Cross Timbers terrain retains its historic character as cattle country.

name: King's Landing, MI
description: King's Landing was a small Michigan Upper Peninsula community along the shore of Lake Superior in Alger County that served the limited logging and fishing economy of this rugged Lake Superior coastline in the late nineteenth century. The community never grew beyond a small cluster of buildings serving the maritime and timber industries, and when those industries declined the community dissolved. The spectacular Lake Superior shoreline where King's Landing sat is now within or near Pictured Rocks National Lakeshore, which was established in 1966 as America's first national lakeshore. The national lakeshore designation protects the area but prohibits metal detecting, though the remarkable shoreline geology draws visitors from across the country.

name: Monte Cristo, WA
description: Monte Cristo was a silver and gold mining camp perched at 2,800 feet in the Cascade Mountains of Snohomish County, Washington, booming in the 1890s when rich ore discoveries drew hundreds of miners to the remote mountain valley accessible only by a railroad that had to conquer some of the most challenging terrain in the Pacific Northwest. The town had hotels, saloons, a concentrator mill, and a thriving commercial district during its brief peak in 1894-1897, but a catastrophic flood in 1897 severely damaged the town and railroad, and the mines never fully recovered. Several historic structures survive in the valley, now accessible via hiking trail since the mining road was converted to a trail through the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest. The surrounding mine dumps, tailing piles, and old building sites on national forest land make this a fascinating hiking destination, though artifact removal on national forest land requires a permit.

name: Gold Town, CA
description: Gold Town was a small placer gold mining camp in Tuolumne County, California in the Mother Lode country of the western Sierra Nevada foothills that developed during the California Gold Rush era of the 1850s, one of hundreds of camps that sprang up along gold-bearing Sierra Nevada streams. Like most placer mining camps, Gold Town faded rapidly once the accessible surface gold was worked out, with miners moving on to newer strikes elsewhere in the Mother Lode. The surrounding Sierra Nevada foothills are now a mix of private land and state and national forest, with many former Gold Rush sites on private cattle ranches or within forest boundaries. Metal detecting in former California Gold Rush era sites can yield period coins, mining equipment, and personal items.

name: Oakwood, KS
description: Oakwood was a small farming community in Coffey County, Kansas that platted during the great homesteading era of the 1870s-1880s, briefly supporting a post office and local commercial services for surrounding agricultural operations in the Neosho River watershed area of east-central Kansas. The community never developed adequate rail connections and was absorbed into the surrounding agricultural landscape within a generation of its founding. The site today is private farmland in Coffey County's productive agricultural territory. Kansas homestead-era communities like Oakwood can yield period artifacts — including pre-Civil War era tokens and coins from early territorial settlers — with proper landowner permission.

name: Gunn, WY
description: Gunn was a small Wyoming community in Natrona County in the Thunder Basin region of northeastern Wyoming that developed briefly as a ranch supply and post office community in the late nineteenth century during the cattle ranching era on the High Plains. The harsh Wyoming climate, periodic drought, and marginal nature of the high-desert ranching economy combined to make permanent community establishment extremely difficult in this region. The site today is private ranchland in the sagebrush steppe of central Wyoming. Former Wyoming ranching communities from the 1880s-1900s can occasionally yield cowboy-era artifacts including leather hardware, horseshoes, and domestic items from the ranch era.

name: Barnwell, CA
description: Barnwell was a small California desert community in San Bernardino County in the Mojave Desert's eastern reaches near the Nevada border, developing as a railroad water stop and mining support community along the Atlantic & Pacific Railroad (later Santa Fe) in the 1880s. The town served as a supply point for surrounding desert mining districts including the Providence Mountains region, and had a post office, stores, and a hotel at its brief peak. As the mining districts faded and railroad technology reduced the need for water stops, Barnwell declined rapidly and was abandoned by the early twentieth century. The Mojave Desert landscape around Barnwell is managed by the Bureau of Land Management and offers open access for exploration of the historic mining and railroad era landscape.

name: Goffs, CA
description: Goffs was a community in San Bernardino County, California along historic Route 66 in the Mojave Desert that served as a railroad station and small community on the National Old Trails Road, later Route 66, providing water, fuel, and limited services to desert travelers in the early automobile era. The community's Schoolhouse, built in 1914, has been restored by the Mojave Desert Heritage and Cultural Association and serves as the organization's headquarters and a museum of desert history. Interstate 40 bypassed Route 66 through this section of the Mojave, leaving Goffs accessible only via the original highway alignment. The restored schoolhouse and surrounding desert landscape make Goffs a worthwhile stop for Route 66 enthusiasts and desert history researchers.

name: Homer, CA
description: Homer was a small California mining community in Mono County along Bodie Creek in the eastern Sierra Nevada, developing in the 1860s-1880s as a satellite settlement in the greater Bodie mining district that served nearby mining claims and provided modest commercial services to miners working the area's gold and silver deposits. The community faded as the Bodie district's main camp overshadowed surrounding satellites, and the town of Homer dissolved as miners consolidated their activities at the more prosperous Bodie site nearby. The surrounding Mono County landscape includes Bodie State Historic Park and extensive BLM land. The arid Sierra Nevada high desert terrain preserves historic artifacts well, and surrounding BLM land (outside the state park) may offer opportunities with proper permits.

name: Forest City, UT
description: Forest City was a small silver and lead mining camp in Juab County, Utah in the Tintic Mining District, established in the 1870s as one of several camps that served the prolific ore deposits of the eastern Tintic Mountains, which would eventually yield hundreds of millions of dollars in precious metals over several decades of mining. The camp existed primarily to house miners working nearby claims and declined as operations consolidated at larger Tintic District centers like Eureka and Silver City. The Tintic Mining District is one of Utah's most historically significant mining regions, and the surrounding Juab County landscape contains extensive mine dumps and historic mining infrastructure. Much of the Tintic District land is now managed by the BLM or is private mining claim land with variable access.

name: Colton, UT
description: Colton was a small Carbon County, Utah mining community along Price River Pass at the western edge of the Wasatch Plateau, serving as a coal mining and railroad support community along the Denver and Rio Grande Western Railroad's mainline through the Price River Canyon. The town existed primarily as a railroad helper station and modest coal mining community from the 1880s through the early twentieth century before declining as rail operations were modernized and the need for helper engines reduced. The Carbon County area remains active coal mining country today, and many former mining community sites exist on private mining land or alongside active railroad rights-of-way. Former railroad helper station communities in this region can yield railroad hardware and early twentieth century domestic artifacts.

name: Clifford Eight, IL
description: Clifford Eight was a small rural community in Iroquois County, Illinois in the flat agricultural belt of northeastern Illinois, another of the region's many short-lived post office communities that platted during the height of Illinois's agricultural settlement in the 1870s-1880s without ever securing the railroad connections needed for permanent commercial viability. The site today is actively farmed cropland in Iroquois County, one of Illinois's most productive agricultural counties. Farm-era detecting in Iroquois County with landowner permission can yield Victorian-era coinage and homestead hardware from the settlement period.

name: Parker City, IL
description: Parker City was a small Iroquois County, Illinois farming community that briefly held a post office during the late nineteenth century while serving the surrounding prairie agricultural operations, but failed to develop the economic base needed to survive the competitive landscape of Illinois's post-railroad rural economy. The site has been absorbed into the surrounding agricultural landscape of Iroquois County. Former post office community sites in Iroquois County are typically private farmland but can be productive detecting sites with permission.

name: Berlin, NV
description: Berlin was a well-preserved Nevada ghost town in Nye County in the Shoshone Mountains, established in the 1890s as a silver and gold mining camp that operated through the early 1910s and then fell silent, its structures abandoned intact to the high-desert climate rather than demolished or burned. The town's remarkable preservation — including the ore processing mill, manager's house, and various outbuildings — earned it a place within Berlin-Ichthyosaur State Park, which also protects a remarkable deposit of Triassic-era ichthyosaur fossils nearby. The state park has made Berlin one of Nevada's most accessible and well-interpreted ghost towns, with guided tours available during the season. Metal detecting is prohibited within state park boundaries, but the preserved townsite and surrounding history make this a must-visit destination in the central Nevada mining landscape.

name: Centerview, KS
description: Centerview was a small farming community in Linn County, Kansas that developed during the post-Civil War settlement of east-central Kansas's fertile Marais des Cygnes River valley, briefly supporting a post office and local agricultural commerce before the railroad network established in neighboring communities captured the regional trade. The Linn County region in southeastern Kansas has a notable Civil War history as a site of border warfare, and the surrounding landscape contains several historically significant Civil War-era sites. The former Centerview site is private farmland in Linn County. Kansas Civil War border region communities can yield mid-nineteenth century coins and military personal items alongside standard homestead-era artifacts.

name: Heineman, WI
description: Heineman was a small Wisconsin farming community in Sauk County in the Baraboo Hills region, one of the state's most geologically distinctive areas with ancient quartzite ridges rising dramatically from the surrounding farmland. The community existed as a modest agricultural service point during Wisconsin's settlement era and faded as the county's commercial activities consolidated at larger centers like Baraboo. Sauk County is home to Baraboo, where the Ringling Brothers Circus wintered, and the region has a rich history connected to both agriculture and entertainment. The surrounding Sauk County landscape includes Parfrey's Glen State Natural Area and Devils Lake State Park, making it a popular outdoor destination today.

name: Butler, TN
description: Butler was a thriving agricultural and commercial community in Carter County, Tennessee in the Watauga River valley in the northeastern corner of the state, established in the 1800s and growing to include churches, stores, a post office, and several hundred residents serving the surrounding mountain farming economy. The town was condemned and flooded in 1948 when the Tennessee Valley Authority completed Watauga Dam, creating Watauga Lake — one of the TVA's high-mountain reservoirs in the Unaka Mountains. Residents were displaced and the town was completely inundated, though the TVA cemetery relocation program moved most of the town's graves to higher ground. During exceptional drought years, the upper lake arms have revealed portions of the old road network and foundations, and the TVA-managed reservoir offers public access along its scenic shoreline.

name: Kwigiumpainukamiut, AK
description: Kwigiumpainukamiut was a traditional Yup'ik Eskimo village on the Bering Sea coast of Alaska whose very long name reflects the Yup'ik language's descriptive place-naming tradition, a community of indigenous Alaskans who lived along this remote stretch of the Alaska coastline for generations sustained by subsistence hunting, fishing, and gathering. The village was abandoned in the twentieth century as Alaska Native communities throughout rural Alaska were relocated by government programs or chose to relocate to larger communities with access to schools and services. The remote Bering Sea coast location means the site is accessible only by small plane or boat. Indigenous Alaskan village sites are protected under the Native American Graves Protection and Repatriation Act, and artifact collection is prohibited.

name: Santa Claus, AZ
description: Santa Claus was a Yavapai County, Arizona roadside attraction and real estate development scheme launched in 1937 along US Route 93 by Harry Miller, who built a small village themed around Christmas imagery — including a post office that operated as 'Santa Claus, Arizona' and received thousands of holiday mail pieces annually from across the country. The venture was intended to anchor a larger real estate development but the remote desert location on the road between Phoenix and Las Vegas never attracted enough buyers, and the business struggled through multiple ownership changes. The last of the Christmas-themed buildings closed in 2006, leaving a collection of decaying holiday structures slowly crumbling in the Sonoran Desert. The accessible roadside ruins make for an odd but memorable stop on the US 93 corridor.

name: Youngberg, AZ
description: Youngberg was a tiny Arizona community in Yavapai County that existed as a minor agricultural and ranching settlement along a desert watercourse in the Prescott area in the early to mid-twentieth century, never developing into a significant community and leaving little documentary or physical record of its existence. The Arizona central highlands landscape around the Prescott area has seen extensive development in the modern era, and many former small ranch communities have been absorbed into suburban or recreational land uses. The surrounding Prescott National Forest and private ranch holdings dominate the landscape in this region of central Arizona.

name: Animas Forks, CO
