The remote West Texas location, extreme heat, scarce water, and the constant wind that defines the Permian Basin landscape made permanent community development extremely difficult, and Orla's population has always been minimal. The Pecos River valley around Orla today is dominated by oil and gas infrastructure from the Permian Basin's massive energy industry. Former ranch and railroad communities in the trans-Pecos region can yield interesting frontier-era artifacts with landowner permission.

name: Gleeson, AZ
description: Gleeson was a small Cochise County, Arizona copper and silver mining community in the Dragoon Mountains of southeastern Arizona, established in the late 1890s near the rich Turquoise Mine and other copper deposits that made this corner of Arizona Territory one of the late Victorian era's productive mineral districts. The town had a post office, jail, hospital, and several commercial buildings serving the mining population through the early twentieth century before the mines declined in the 1920s-1930s and the population dispersed. The adobe ruins of Gleeson — including the remarkably preserved hospital, jail, and schoolhouse — are accessible on BLM land, making this one of southeastern Arizona's most rewarding ghost town destinations for metal detectorists and history enthusiasts. The accessible BLM land and well-preserved adobe ruins make Gleeson one of the more productive detecting sites in Cochise County.

name: Steins, NM
description: Steins was a small Hidalgo County, New Mexico railroad ghost town in the Peloncillo Mountains along the Southern Pacific Railroad mainline in the bootheel of New Mexico, serving as a section house community and modest supply point for travelers crossing this remote corner of the Southwest from the 1880s through the mid-twentieth century. The town had a post office, store, and railroad facilities, and was notable for being on the old Butterfield Overland Mail route that crossed this mountain pass in the late 1850s — one of the most historically significant transportation corridors in the antebellum Southwest. Steins has been privately acquired and developed as a privately operated ghost town attraction with original structures, artifacts, and interpretive displays. Entry requires paying a modest fee to the private owners who have preserved the site's remarkable collection of early twentieth-century railroad and small-town artifacts.

name: Delamar, NV
description: Delamar was a significant silver mining boomtown in Owyhee County, Idaho in the Owyhee Mountains, established in 1889 after Captain Joseph Delamar purchased the struggling mines and used modern stamp mill technology to process the ore at a scale that transformed the camp into a proper town of 3,000 people by the mid-1890s. The intensive milling of the siliceous ore created a cloud of deadly silica dust that killed hundreds of miners and workers in a phenomenon so widespread that Delamar became known as 'the widowmaker' — an early and tragic example of industrial occupational disease. The mines closed in 1900 when the ore was exhausted, and Delamar was rapidly abandoned. The remote Owyhee Mountains site is accessible via dirt roads on BLM land, with extensive mine waste dumps, building foundations, and old stamp mill ruins visible in the landscape.

name: Ballarat, CA
description: Ballarat was a Mojave Desert ghost town in Inyo County, California in Panamint Valley west of Death Valley, established in the 1890s as a supply base for the numerous mining camps in the surrounding Panamint and Argus ranges, eventually developing into a modest desert community with a hotel, saloon, and post office that served as the last resupply point before the brutal Death Valley crossing. The town was associated with the notorious outlaw and prospector Charles Manson, who used the Barker Ranch in the Panamint Mountains above Ballarat as a hideout before his 1969 arrest. Today only a few crumbling adobe walls and a private general store operated by a caretaker remain at Ballarat, which is accessible via a dirt road on BLM land. The surrounding Panamint Valley and Argus Range offer extensive BLM land for prospecting and historical exploration.

name: Freda, ND
description: Freda was a small Houghton County, Michigan copper mining community on the Keweenaw Peninsula in the Lake Superior copper district, established in the late nineteenth century as a residential community for workers in the nearby copper mines and stamp mills of the Quincy and Hancock area. The community occupied lakeside land on the Portage Lake shoreline and was eventually acquired as the copper industry consolidated, with remaining structures demolished or absorbed into the broader Houghton County landscape. The Keweenaw Peninsula today is recognized as a National Heritage Area celebrating the world's greatest copper mining district, and the Quincy Mine and other preserved industrial heritage sites offer remarkable insight into the immigrant workers who built this community.

name: Keomuku, HI
description: Keomuku was a plantation-era community on the eastern coast of Lanai, the smallest of Hawaii's inhabited islands, established in the 1890s when the Maunalei Sugar Company attempted to grow sugarcane on Lanai's eastern coast — one of several ill-fated agricultural ventures that preceded the island's twentieth century transformation into a pineapple plantation. The venture failed within a decade, reportedly cursed after workers dismantled a heiau (ancient Hawaiian temple) to use its stones for a railroad. The remains of Ka Lanakila Church, built by workers in 1903, still stand at Keomuku and have been intermittently maintained as a historic landmark. The site is accessible via a rough four-wheel-drive coastal road, with the old church ruins and former plantation landscape offering a quiet glimpse of Lanai's pre-pineapple history.

name: Belmont, NV
description: Belmont was a significant Nevada silver mining boomtown in Nye County in the Monitor Valley, established in 1865 after rich silver discoveries and growing to over 2,000 people by the late 1860s, serving as Nye County's seat of government with a courthouse, newspaper, and all the infrastructure of a frontier silver town. The town declined in the 1880s as silver prices fell and the mines reached depths where extraction became uneconomical, and the county seat was moved to Tonopah in 1905 when that community's remarkable silver-gold discovery eclipsed everything else in the region. The remarkably preserved ruins of Belmont — including the standing walls of the old courthouse, the Cosmopolitan Saloon, and several other structures — are accessible on BLM and state land. The accessible ruins make Belmont one of central Nevada's most historically significant and rewarding ghost town destinations.

name: Unionville, NV
description: Unionville was an early Nevada silver mining camp in Pershing County in the Humboldt Mountains, established in 1861 during the initial Nevada silver rush and notable for being the site where young Samuel Clemens — not yet 'Mark Twain' — tried his hand at silver mining in 1861-1862 before abandoning the shovel in favor of the pen. Clemens's hilarious account of his failed mining adventure appeared in 'Roughing It' (1872), one of American literature's great comic accounts of western frontier experience. The town had a post office, stores, and several hundred residents during its peak years before the silver played out and the population dispersed. A handful of structures survive in the narrow Buena Vista Canyon, and the surrounding Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forest offers access to the greater area.

name: St. Elmo, CO
description: St. Elmo was a Colorado ghost town in Chaffee County in the Sawatch Range at 10,000 feet elevation, booming in the 1880s when the Alpine Tunnel railroad and silver mining combined to create a town of over 2,000 people with hotels, saloons, a post office, and all the amenities of a prosperous Victorian mountain community — including a school where the legendary 'Dirty Annie' Stark ran the general store into the 1950s, long after everyone else had left. The silver crash of 1893 decimated the economy, the Alpine Tunnel closed in 1910, and St. Elmo slowly depopulated through the early twentieth century. Today over 40 original buildings survive in remarkable condition, accessible via a paved road to this remarkably intact Colorado ghost town. Metal detecting is not permitted on the state-recognized historic site, but the surrounding Gunnison National Forest offers exploration opportunities.

name: Batsto Village, NJ
description: Batsto Village in Burlington County, New Jersey is a remarkably preserved eighteenth and nineteenth-century iron furnace and glassmaking village in the Pine Barrens, established in 1766 and operating through the Revolutionary War — producing bog iron cannon and other military hardware — before transitioning to glass production in the mid-nineteenth century as the bog iron deposits were depleted. The village's approximately 33 historic structures including the ironmaster's mansion, workers' cottages, sawmill, and gristmill have been preserved as Batsto Village within Wharton State Forest, one of the finest examples of an intact early American industrial village in the Northeast. The site is managed by the New Jersey Division of Parks and Forestry with regular interpretive programs and tours. Metal detecting is not permitted within the state forest historic site.

name: Silver City, ID
description: Silver City was Idaho's premier silver and gold mining boomtown, perched at 6,200 feet in the Owyhee Mountains of Owyhee County, booming from the mid-1860s through the 1870s when the Poorman and War Eagle Mountain mines produced millions of dollars in precious metals and the town swelled to over 2,500 residents with hotels, a newspaper, churches, and a full range of frontier commercial and entertainment establishments. The deep underground mines continued to produce through the early twentieth century with various revivals, and Silver City had year-round residents into the late twentieth century. Today the community is managed by the Idaho Department of Lands as a historic townsite, with over 70 original structures surviving and accessible via a rough mountain road. The surrounding Owyhee Mountains BLM land is rich with mining history, and the accessible public land allows exploration beyond the historic townsite itself.

name: Ashcroft, CO
description: Ashcroft was a Colorado silver mining camp in Pitkin County in the Castle Creek valley south of Aspen at 9,500 feet elevation, founded in 1880 and briefly rivaling Aspen itself with a population that reached 2,500 at its peak during the silver boom of the 1880s, complete with multiple hotels, saloons, and a post office. The silver crash of 1893 destroyed Ashcroft's economy, and the population had declined to virtually nothing by 1900, though a few die-hard residents remained through the early twentieth century. In the 1930s the Chicago Tribune publisher's son Laurence Cowles attempted a ski resort development that briefly revived interest, but the venture failed. Today Ashcroft is preserved as a ghost town attraction managed by the Aspen Historical Society and is accessible via paved Castle Creek Road, with several original log structures maintained for public interpretation.

name: Johnsonville, IN
description: Johnsonville was a small Harrison County, Indiana community along the Wabash River drainage in west-central Indiana that platted during the early settlement era of the Indiana frontier in the 1820s-1830s, briefly supporting a post office and local agricultural commerce before larger, railroad-connected communities in Harrison County absorbed the regional trade. Harrison County was settled early in Indiana's history and has a significant connection to the state's territorial and early statehood government. The site today is private agricultural land in Harrison County. Former Indiana frontier-era communities from the 1820s-1840s can yield early American coinage and personal items reflective of the Ohio Valley settlement era.

name: Benmore, UT
description: Benmore was a small Millard County, Utah community in the Sevier Desert of central Utah, briefly holding a post office and serving the limited ranching and dry farming activities in this arid basin landscape in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The Great Basin landscape of Millard County — one of Utah's largest and most sparsely populated counties — is characterized by salt flats, desert shrubland, and the scattered mountain ranges that create the distinctive Basin and Range topography. Most small communities in Millard County that attempted dryland farming in the Sevier Desert failed to sustain themselves given the insufficient and unreliable precipitation. The site today is private ranch or BLM desert land in the broad Sevier Desert basin.

name: Pithole City, PA
description: Pithole City was one of the most spectacular boomtowns in American history, exploding from empty Pennsylvania farmland in Venango County to a city of 15,000 people in just six months after a January 1865 oil well struck a massive flow — making it the first major American oil gusher in the post-Civil War petroleum boom. At its frenzied peak, Pithole had 57 hotels, dozens of saloons, a daily newspaper, a post office that briefly ranked third in Pennsylvania by volume, and a pipeline to the Oil Creek railroad — one of the world's first oil pipelines. By 1866 the oil flow declined and the population vanished nearly as fast as it had arrived, with the buildings dismantled and sold for lumber. Today the Pithole site is maintained by the Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission, with cellar depressions and a visitor center marking what was once a city of thousands.

name: Buffalo City, NC
description: Buffalo City was an early nineteenth-century North Carolina community in Wilkes County in the Blue Ridge Mountain foothills, developing as a local agricultural and commercial center during the early settlement of western North Carolina before larger county towns with better road connections captured the regional trade. Western North Carolina's mountain communities carry a rich Scots-Irish cultural heritage and a significant connection to the American Revolution-era backcountry conflicts between Tories and Patriots that shaped the region's history. The Wilkes County landscape today is private farmland and forest, and former community sites in this region can yield Revolutionary and early Federal-era artifacts.

name: New Birmingham, TX
description: New Birmingham was a planned industrial city in Morris County, Texas promoted aggressively in the early 1890s by land speculators who claimed the site had iron ore deposits of Pennsylvania-grade quality that would make it 'the Pittsburgh of the South' — attracting significant investment and a furnace operation before the ore proved to be of insufficient quality and quantity. The iron furnace was built and briefly operated before the scheme collapsed in the mid-1890s, leaving investors with worthless land and the town's few permanent residents with little recourse. The ruins of the New Birmingham iron furnace were later preserved as part of what is now New Birmingham City Park in Morris County, with the dramatic stone furnace stack remaining as one of East Texas's most unusual industrial relics. The accessible park site makes this one of Texas's more interesting industrial ghost town destinations.

name: Kouns, TX
description: Kouns was a tiny Lipscomb County, Texas community in the Texas Panhandle High Plains, one of the many small farming and ranching service communities that briefly supported a post office during the dryland homesteading boom of the early twentieth century before drought, agricultural hardship, and the Dust Bowl of the 1930s eliminated the economic rationale for such communities. The Llano Estacado landscape of the Texas Panhandle was settled late in American history and was always marginal for dryland farming without irrigation. The site today is private ranchland or farmland in the remote Canadian River basin country of the Texas Panhandle.

name: National City, IL
description: National City was a small Macoupin County, Illinois farming community along the Illinois River corridor in west-central Illinois that briefly held a post office in the nineteenth century while serving surrounding agricultural operations in this section of the Prairie State. Macoupin County has significant Coal Age history from both the geology of its rich coal seams and the labor history of the coal mining communities that developed along the county's northern boundary. The site today is private farmland in the flat to rolling terrain of Macoupin County. Former post office communities in this region of Illinois can yield agricultural and homestead artifacts from the 1860s-1880s.

name: Cumpressco, FL
description: Cumpressco was a small Hernando County, Florida community in the interior scrub and pine flatwood terrain of the central Florida peninsula, existing briefly as an agricultural service point and post office during the late nineteenth century Florida pioneer era when settlers attempted to establish farming operations in the challenging interior landscape of the state. The interior Florida scrubland is notoriously poor agricultural territory — sandy, nutrient-poor, and prone to drought — and many small pioneer communities in counties like Hernando failed to sustain themselves. The site today is private land or state-managed scrubland in the Hernando County interior.

name: Idlewild, UT
description: Idlewild was a small Millard County, Utah community in the Sevier Valley of central Utah, one of the modest Mormon pioneer agricultural settlements that attempted to farm the desert valleys of Millard County using irrigation from mountain streams. The Sevier Valley farming communities faced chronic water shortages and the challenges of high desert agriculture, and many small communities like Idlewild were abandoned as farming proved unviable or populations consolidated in larger valley towns. The Millard County Sevier Valley communities represent the extended reach of Brigham Young's vision for populating every potentially farmable valley in the Great Basin, regardless of the agricultural odds.

name: Southern Cross, MT
description: Southern Cross was a small Granite County, Montana gold and silver mining community in the Flint Creek Range, established in the 1870s-1880s during the great Montana silver and gold rushes that brought prospectors to every accessible mountain range in the territory. The mine produced modest quantities of precious metals before declining ore grades made operations uneconomical, and the community faded as miners moved to more productive areas. The Granite County mining landscape around Philipsburg is historically rich, containing the famous Granite Mine — one of Montana's greatest silver producers — and numerous smaller operations like Southern Cross. The surrounding Deerlodge National Forest and BLM land in Granite County offer access to the historic mining landscape.

name: Rimini, MT
description: Rimini was a small Jefferson County, Montana mining community in the Big Belt Mountains near Helena, established in the 1860s-1870s in the gold and silver-bearing formations of the mountains east of Montana's capital city. The mining district produced intermittently through the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, and the community existed to serve the various mining operations in the area. The area became environmentally significant for a different reason — lead and arsenic contamination from historic mining operations in Rimini Creek affected downstream water supplies and led to an EPA Superfund designation for cleanup of the mining legacy. The cleanup status affects access and artifact recovery in the area.

name: Elkhorn, MT
description: Elkhorn was a silver mining community in Jefferson County, Montana in the Elkhorn Mountains south of Boulder, established in the 1870s and producing significant silver wealth through the 1880s — particularly from the Elkhorn Mine that produced over $14 million in silver during its peak years. The town had a population of 2,500 at its height with a hotel, several saloons, a school, and the Fraternity Hall — a two-story structure that became one of Montana's most photographed ghost town buildings. Montana State Parks manages Elkhorn Ghost Town as a state park with two original structures preserved, and the remaining mine workings and townsite are accessible via a dirt road south of Boulder. Metal detecting is prohibited within the state park boundaries.

name: Fairfax, WA
description: Fairfax was a small coal mining community in Pierce County, Washington in the Carbon River valley on the western slope of the Cascades, established in the early twentieth century to house miners working the Carbon Hill coal mines that supplied fuel to Puget Sound industries and the Northern Pacific Railroad. The community had company housing, a school, and basic services for the mining families who worked the underground coal seams in this rugged Cascade Mountain valley. The Carbon River valley today is part of Mount Rainier National Park's carbon river area, accessible via a trail that follows the old Carbon River Road (closed to vehicles). The national park designation prohibits metal detecting, but the dramatic Cascade rainforest setting makes this a spectacular hiking destination with significant natural and industrial heritage.

name: Melmont, WA
description: Melmont was another Carbon River coal mining community in Pierce County, Washington along the Carbon River upstream of Fairfax, established in the late nineteenth century as one of several coal camps that exploited the Carbon Hill coal seams of the western Cascade foothills. The community had company housing and basic services, and like Fairfax was eventually absorbed into the Carbon River landscape when coal mining operations ceased and the community was abandoned. The Carbon River area's coal mining heritage is preserved in historical accounts and the landscape itself, though the national park status means the accessible areas are managed for natural and historical preservation rather than recreational artifact recovery.

name: White City, CA
description: White City was a small Kern County, California community in the southern Sierra Nevada foothills or San Joaquin Valley that existed briefly as an agricultural or service community in the early twentieth century California landscape. The Central Valley and surrounding foothills of Kern County have been intensively developed for agriculture and petroleum production, and many small early twentieth century communities have been absorbed into the broader agricultural and industrial landscape of what is now one of California's most productive oil and farming regions.

name: Montezuma, KS (Old)
description: Montezuma (Old) was the original county seat of Gray County, Kansas in the short grass prairie of the southwestern Kansas plains, established during the great land boom of the late 1880s that brought hundreds of settlers to western Kansas with inflated promises of agricultural prosperity in a region that receives barely 15 inches of rainfall per year. The county seat was relocated and Montezuma declined as the devastating drought and agricultural bust of the early 1890s — the same period that inspired the Populist political movement — drove most settlers from western Kansas. The Gray County landscape today is working agricultural land devoted to wheat farming and feedlot cattle operations, with the original Montezuma site now a small surviving community adjacent to the historic location.

name: Sumica, FL
description: Sumica was a small Hillsborough County, Florida community in the interior of the Florida peninsula that existed briefly as a farming or turpentine community during the early twentieth century settlement of central Florida's pine flatwood interior. The turpentine industry was a major driver of early Florida settlement, with crews harvesting longleaf pine resin from the vast pine forests of the interior, creating a network of small camps and communities across central and north Florida. The Hillsborough County landscape today is dominated by the greater Tampa metropolitan area in its western portions and agricultural land and wildlife management areas in the eastern interior. Former turpentine era communities in central Florida's private rural land are typically inaccessible to recreational detectorists.

name: Ingersoll
description: Ingersoll was a small Wisconsin community briefly existing as a local post office and farming service point in the agricultural landscape of the Upper Midwest, one of many modest rural communities that platted during Wisconsin's peak agricultural settlement era in the 1870s-1880s. The exact county location and history of this entry is somewhat uncertain, but it represents the broad pattern of Wisconsin's small agricultural communities that failed to achieve railroad connections and were subsequently absorbed into the surrounding farmland. Former Wisconsin rural post office communities can yield late Victorian agricultural and domestic artifacts with landowner permission.

name: Waldstein, AR
description: Waldstein was a small Arkansas community in the Ozark Mountain region of the state, one of many modest hill country settlements that developed during the late nineteenth century Arkansas settlement era as families moved into the rugged Ozark plateau seeking farming and timbering opportunities in the cedar and hardwood uplands. Arkansas's Ozark region communities were shaped by Scotch-Irish and German immigrant settlement and had a rich folk culture that persisted long after the communities themselves faded. The remote Ozark landscape of western Arkansas today is a mix of private farmland, national forest, and state parks, with many former small community sites accessible within the Ozara National Forest.

name: Renno, SC
description: Renno was a small South Carolina community likely in the Piedmont or coastal plain region, existing as a minor agricultural and post office community during the cotton-era farming economy of the nineteenth century South Carolina. The state's antebellum cotton culture created a landscape of small communities and crossroads serving the plantation and small farm economy, many of which declined precipitously after the Civil War when the plantation system collapsed. Former South Carolina farming communities from the antebellum and Reconstruction eras can yield interesting period artifacts from both before and after the war.

name: Auraria, GA
description: Auraria was Georgia's first gold rush town in Lumpkin County in the Blue Ridge Mountains, established after gold was discovered there in 1832 — nearly two decades before the California Gold Rush — making this corner of north Georgia the site of America's first significant gold rush. The town briefly had a population of several thousand miners before being overshadowed by the nearby town of Dahlonega (which had the federal mint) and eventually declined as the surface gold was worked out. Today Auraria is a National Register Historic District in Lumpkin County, with some original structures surviving and significant archaeological potential from the gold rush era. The surrounding Lumpkin County landscape includes gold panning opportunities along the Chestatee River that draw prospectors from across the Southeast.

name: Comet, MT
description: Comet was a small Silver Bow County, Montana silver mining community in the Pioneer Mountains south of Butte, established in the 1880s as part of the great Montana silver rush that made Silver Bow County one of the richest mining regions in North America. The Montana silver mining districts surrounding Butte produced enormous wealth before declining silver prices gutted the industry, and many small satellite camps like Comet faded as the big Butte copper mines came to dominate the regional economy. The Pioneer Mountains landscape is now largely managed by the Bureau of Land Management, and old mine workings and camp foundations from the silver era are scattered across the mountain terrain. The BLM land in this region is accessible for historical exploration within permitted parameters.

name: Whitney, OR
description: Whitney was a small Baker County, Oregon ranching and farming community in the Eagle Valley of the Blue Mountains, established in the late nineteenth century as a supply and post office community for surrounding agricultural operations in the rugged terrain of eastern Oregon's basin and range country. The community existed as a modest service point until the regional economy consolidated in larger Baker County centers. Baker County's mining history, particularly the gold discoveries in the Cornucopia and Unity areas, make eastern Oregon a historically rich detecting region. The surrounding Wallowa-Whitman National Forest and BLM lands offer extensive public access for history enthusiasts in this remote but scenic corner of Oregon.

name: Knox Mills, WI
description: Knox Mills was a small Wisconsin farming and milling community likely in the Fox River watershed of the central Wisconsin landscape, established as a mill and agricultural service point during Wisconsin's great settlement era and fading as the regional economy consolidated in larger milling centers with railroad access. Wisconsin's many small mill communities of the nineteenth century were the backbone of the agricultural economy, processing grain from surrounding farms before the industry was reorganized around larger, more efficient railroad-served mills. Former Wisconsin mill sites and their surrounding communities are typically on private agricultural land today.

name: Anchorage, WI
description: Anchorage was a small Wisconsin community — distinct from the Alaska city — likely a farming or logging community in the northern or central Wisconsin lake country that existed briefly as a post office community during the Wisconsin settlement era of the 1870s-1890s. The Wisconsin northland was simultaneously being logged at an industrial pace and settled by farming families during this period, creating a chaotic pattern of community formation and abandonment as the timber played out and farming proved difficult in the sandy soils left by glacial outwash. Former Wisconsin northland communities that attempted farming after logging typically left only cellar depressions and stone walls in the recovering second-growth forest.

name: Army Lake, WI
description: Army Lake was a small Wisconsin community in Waukesha County in the Kettle Moraine lake district of southeastern Wisconsin, a resort or farming community that developed around one of the region's many glacial lakes during the late nineteenth century when Wisconsin's natural lake landscape attracted both farmers and resort-seekers from Milwaukee and Chicago. The Kettle Moraine region of Waukesha County is now largely developed as part of the greater Milwaukee suburban area, and former rural communities in this region have generally been absorbed into residential development. The Ice Age National Scientific Reserve and Kettle Moraine State Forest preserve portions of the original natural landscape.

name: Ashford, WI
description: Ashford was a small Wisconsin community in Fond du Lac County in the Kettle Moraine region, existing as a farming settlement in the rolling glacial terrain of eastern Wisconsin during the great agricultural settlement era of the mid-to-late nineteenth century. Fond du Lac County was settled by German, Irish, and Yankee farmers in the 1840s-1860s, creating a rich agricultural landscape that has been continuously farmed for over 150 years. Former Wisconsin farming communities in Fond du Lac County typically survive only as the names of townships, with the original community sites now private farmland. Detecting in former Wisconsin farming communities from the antebellum period can yield early American coinage and homestead artifacts.

name: Bagdad, WI
description: Bagdad, Wisconsin was a small farming community in Portage County in central Wisconsin that existed briefly as a post office community in the agricultural settlement of the Wisconsin interior during the late nineteenth century. Portage County's central Wisconsin landscape is dominated by the sandy plains deposited by glacial Lake Wisconsin, making farming challenging in the drier areas — a factor that contributed to many small communities' difficulties in sustaining population. Stevens Point is the dominant Portage County center, and most small farming communities in the county consolidated toward the railroad towns in the latter nineteenth century.

name: Bohri, WI
description: Bohri was a small Wisconsin community — possibly a Scandinavian immigrant settlement — in the northern or central Wisconsin logging and farming country that existed briefly during the great Wisconsin northern land rush of the 1880s-1900s. Many Wisconsin northland communities bore names reflecting the origins of their settlers — Norwegian, Swedish, Finnish, and German immigrant communities spread across Wisconsin's cutover lands after the timber was exhausted. The failed cutover farming movement of Wisconsin's early twentieth century left dozens of such communities abandoned when soil conditions proved too poor for sustained agriculture.

name: Doaksville, OK
description: Doaksville was the capital of the Choctaw Nation during the antebellum era, a significant Native American political and commercial center in the southeastern part of Indian Territory (now McCurtain County, Oklahoma) that had a population of several thousand in the 1840s-1850s and served as the center of Choctaw government, commerce, and social life after the tribe's forced removal from Mississippi along the Trail of Tears in the 1830s. The town had a Presbyterian mission, several trading posts, and the Choctaw Nation's governmental buildings before Fort Towson — the nearby U.S. Army post — closed and the capital moved to Skullyville and eventually Tuskahoma. Doaksville is most famous as the site where Confederate General Stand Watie, the last Confederate general to surrender, laid down his arms on June 23, 1865. The site is preserved as a historic landmark in McCurtain County.

name: Texola, OK
description: Texola was a small Oklahoma-Texas border community in Beckham County, Oklahoma along historic Route 66, existing on the very edge of the Texas state line as a small but persistent service community for cross-country travelers on the Mother Road. The town's Route 66 heritage included a jail so small it's been called the 'world's smallest jail,' various tourist attractions, and the typical small highway service economy of the Route 66 era that collapsed when Interstate 40 bypassed the community. Today Texola has a handful of residents and several original Route 66-era buildings, making it one of Oklahoma's more accessible Route 66 ghost town experiences. The open, accessible nature of the surviving buildings and the surrounding Beckham County landscape make this a worthwhile stop for Route 66 and Oklahoma history enthusiasts.

name: Frisco, UT
description: Frisco was one of Utah's most spectacular silver mining ghost towns, a Beaver County boom camp in the San Francisco Mountains that reached a population of 6,000 in the early 1880s when the Horn Silver Mine produced some of the richest ore ever found in Utah, yielding $16 million in silver by 1885. The camp had 23 saloons, multiple hotels and stores, and was notorious for its lawlessness — a marshal killed six men on his first night on the job, reportedly hanging them in pairs from the same telegraph pole. The Horn Silver Mine's main shaft collapsed in 1885, triggering a massive cave-in that ended the camp's boom era virtually overnight. The ruins of Frisco — including the remarkably preserved beehive charcoal kilns and scattered building foundations — are accessible on BLM land south of Milford, Utah, making this one of the West's most rewarding ghost town sites for artifact hunters and history enthusiasts.

name: Turret, CO
description: Turret was a small Chaffee County, Colorado gold mining camp in the Turret Mining District in the rugged mountains north of Salida, established in the 1890s after gold discoveries attracted prospectors to the remote canyon country between the Arkansas River and the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. The camp had a post office, several mines, and a small commercial district serving the mining population through the late 1890s-1910s before declining ore values caused most operations to cease. The Turret area is accessible via rough mountain roads from Salida in the Chaffee County high country, with some structures and mine workings visible in the landscape. The surrounding San Isabel National Forest and BLM land in this region of Colorado offers public access for exploration of the historic mining landscape.

name: Tuckerville, CO
description: Tuckerville was a small Huerfano County, Colorado community in the Cucharas River valley of the southern Colorado coalfields, one of several small settlements that served the coal mining economy of the southern Sangre de Cristo region in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The southern Colorado coal camps were culturally diverse — Italian, Slavic, Hispanic, and other immigrant groups creating a rich mosaic of working-class culture in the company towns along the Trinidad coalfield. The dramatic labor battles of 1913-1914 shaped the history of every community in this region, and the memory of Ludlow weighs heavily on the landscape. Former Huerfano County coal communities are largely on private or corporate land in the valleys below the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.

name: Alta, CO
description: Alta was a Ouray County, Colorado silver mining camp in the San Juan Mountains near Telluride, established in the 1870s-1880s during the great San Juan silver rush at nearly 12,000 feet elevation in some of the most spectacular alpine terrain in North America. The camp produced significant quantities of gold and silver from the adjacent Gold King Mine and surrounding claims, supporting a population of several hundred in the harsh high-altitude environment. The Gold King Mine later became notorious in 2015 when an EPA cleanup crew accidentally released three million gallons of mine waste into the Animas River, triggering a major environmental disaster. The site is now partly within private mining claim land and partly accessible via 4WD roads near Ophir Pass, with the spectacular San Juan Mountain setting making it worth the challenging approach.

name: Trojan, CA
description: Trojan, California was a small mining community in Trinity County, California in the rugged Trinity Mountains of far northern California, connected to the gold and silver mining history of the Trinity Alps and Salmon River country that attracted prospectors from the Gold Rush era onward. Trinity County's mining history is extensive — the county produced significant gold through multiple mining eras — and many small camps like Trojan developed and faded alongside the fortunes of individual mines and districts. The Trinity Alps Wilderness and surrounding Shasta-Trinity National Forest cover much of Trinity County's rugged mountain terrain. Former Trinity County mining camp sites on national forest land are accessible for exploration within the permitted parameters for artifact recovery.

name: Trojan, SD
description: Trojan, South Dakota was a small Pennington County, South Dakota community in the Black Hills gold country, one of many small mining camps that developed in the hills surrounding Deadwood and Lead during the great Black Hills Gold Rush beginning in 1876. The Black Hills attracted tens of thousands of prospectors who spread across the region's ponderosa pine-covered hills and gulches seeking the next great gold deposit, creating dozens of short-lived camps like Trojan alongside the major producers. The Black Hills National Forest covers much of the surrounding landscape, and the legacy of Black Hills mining history is one of South Dakota's richest heritage resources. Former Black Hills mining camp sites within national forest land are accessible for surface historical exploration.

name: Heron, MT
description: Heron was a small Sanders County, Montana logging community along the Clark Fork River in the Cabinet Mountains region of northwest Montana, established as a railroad service point and logging camp support community along the Northern Pacific Railroad mainline in the late nineteenth century. The Cabinet Mountains of Sanders County have a rich mining and logging history, and the Clark Fork River valley served as a transportation corridor connecting the mining and logging economy of northwestern Montana to markets in the east. The surrounding Kootenai and Lolo National Forest land makes much of the area publicly accessible, and former logging camp sites in this part of Montana can yield period equipment and domestic artifacts.

name: Encampment, MN
description: Encampment, Minnesota was a small farming community in the Minnesota prairie landscape, one of many brief post office communities that appeared and disappeared during Minnesota's great agricultural settlement era of the 1870s-1890s as the state's rich farmland was rapidly occupied by waves of Scandinavian, German, and other immigrant farmers. Minnesota's precise agricultural grid of townships and sections supported many small communities with post offices serving as nodes in the farm-to-market network, though most lacked railroad connections and were quickly superseded. The Minnesota prairie site today is private farmland in one of the Midwest's most productive agricultural regions.

name: Malmota/Maramba/Marmata, MN
description: Malmota/Maramba/Marmata (Minnesota) was a small Minnesota farming community with a name that appears in historical records under multiple spellings, suggesting a short-lived post office community in the agricultural interior of the state during the late nineteenth century settlement era. The multiple name variations may reflect different proprietors or recording errors in postal records — a common situation in the era before standardized documentation of small rural post offices. The site today is private farmland in the Minnesota agricultural landscape.

name: Burlington, MN
description: Burlington, Minnesota was a small farming community distinct from the many other Burlingtons across America, existing briefly as an agricultural service point in the Minnesota prairie during the great Midwest homesteading era. Minnesota was settled with remarkable speed in the 1850s-1870s by farmers who rapidly occupied the state's extraordinarily rich prairie and forest soils, creating dozens of small communities that competed for railroad access and county seat status. The Burlington site in Minnesota is private farmland today, absorbed into the continuous agricultural landscape of the Upper Midwest.

name: Centralia, OK
description: Centralia was a small Oklahoma community in Carter County or a neighboring county that existed briefly as a farming settlement or crossroads community in the Indian Territory/Oklahoma Territory transitional era of the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The Oklahoma settlement landscape was dramatically reorganized after the Land Runs of 1889 and subsequent years, creating intense competition among new communities for commercial viability. Many small Oklahoma communities like Centralia that lacked railroad connections or favorable geography failed to sustain themselves. The surrounding Oklahoma landscape is working ranch and farm country with private landholdings predominating.

name: Keighley, KS
description: Keighley was a small Comanche County, Kansas agricultural community on the southern Kansas plains in the Arkansas River watershed, one of the many short-lived farming communities that attempted to sustain themselves in the semiarid margins of western Kansas during the dryland homesteading boom of the 1870s-1880s. The combination of insufficient rainfall, harsh winters, and the devastating agricultural bust of the early 1890s — which wiped out most of western Kansas's farming communities — made Keighley's fate typical of the region. The Comanche County landscape today is primarily native shortgrass prairie and cattle ranch country, much of it preserved as the Comanche National Grassland. Former homestead sites on the Comanche National Grassland offer limited access for historical research.

name: Arkalon, KS
description: Arkalon was a small Seward County, Kansas community along the Cimarron River in the extreme southwestern corner of the state, developing briefly during the railroad and farming boom of the late 1880s before the catastrophic drought and agricultural bust of the early 1890s drove most settlers from this marginal corner of the Kansas plains. Southwest Kansas was dubbed 'No Man's Land' and 'The Cimarron Country' by early travelers for its extreme climate and minimal moisture, and many communities in Seward County that briefly appeared during the boom never recovered from the bust. The surrounding Cimarron National Grassland covers much of the southwestern Kansas landscape today, preserving the shortgrass prairie ecosystem and some historical remnants of the homesteading era.

name: Forest Mills, MN
description: Forest Mills was a small Minnesota community likely in the agricultural interior of the state, existing briefly as a mill and farming service community during Minnesota's great settlement era of the 1860s-1880s. Mill communities in Minnesota — particularly those on small streams providing waterpower — often failed when steam-powered mills in railroad-connected towns proved more economical, eliminating the geographic advantage that water power had provided. The Minnesota agricultural landscape today is dominated by the corn and soybean monoculture of modern industrial farming, with former small mill community sites absorbed into private farmland.

name: Ruby, AZ
description: Ruby was an Arizona ghost town in Santa Cruz County near the Mexican border in the Tumacacori Highlands, established as a mining community in the 1870s around lead and zinc deposits in the rugged border mountains, growing to a population of over 1,200 in the 1930s when the Montana Mine produced significant quantities of lead, zinc, and gold. The remote border location made Ruby both isolated and dangerous — the town experienced multiple violent robberies in the 1920s in which the Pearson family who ran the post office and store were brutally murdered. The mine closed in 1941 and Ruby was completely abandoned, and the site is now privately owned and operated as a fee-based ghost town attraction with original buildings remarkably preserved by the dry Arizona climate. Metal detecting at Ruby requires permission from and payment to the private owners.

name: Vulture City, AZ
description: Vulture City was one of Arizona's most productive and historically significant gold mining communities, established in Wickenburg area of Maricopa County in 1863 when Henry Wickenburg discovered the Vulture Mine — eventually one of the richest gold mines in Arizona Territory's history, producing over $200 million in gold by some estimates. The mine and associated town operated on and off for nearly a century, supporting hundreds of workers at peak production periods, and the notorious practice of 'high-grading' (miners stealing ore) was so rampant that the company's assay office and mine buildings featured iron bars on the windows and a hanging tree was reportedly used to deter theft. The Vulture Mine is now preserved as a fee-access ghost town attraction, with original adobe and wood structures remarkably preserved. Metal detecting on-site requires permission from the private owners.

name: Independence, CO
description: Independence was a high-altitude Colorado ghost town in Pitkin County at 11,000 feet in the Elk Mountains near Independence Pass on the Continental Divide, established in 1879 after a gold discovery on July 4th (hence the patriotic name) and briefly growing to 2,000 residents before the easily accessible ore was exhausted within a few years. The extreme altitude and brutal winter conditions — the site receives enormous snowfall and can be snowed in for months — made year-round habitation nearly impossible, and Independence was abandoned by the mid-1880s. The site today is preserved by the Aspen Historical Society with several original log structures, accessible via a short walk from the paved Highway 82 over Independence Pass. The accessible public road and interpretive preservation make Independence one of Colorado's most easily visited high-altitude ghost towns.

name: Uptop, CO
description: Uptop was a small community in Las Animas County, Colorado at the summit of Raton Pass on the Colorado-New Mexico border along the Santa Fe Trail route, existing as a railroad service community along the Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe Railway's critical mountain crossing in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The pass crossing was of immense strategic importance in both commercial and military history — the Santa Fe Trail crossed here, and Civil War forces fought for control of the Raton Pass approach in 1862. The railroad grade over Raton Pass has since been modernized and the service community at the summit is no longer needed. The Trinidad area of Las Animas County has significant coal mining and labor history alongside the Santa Fe Trail heritage.

name: Rodney, MS\n
description: Rodney was an early Mississippi River town in Jefferson County, Mississippi that flourished briefly as a cotton shipping port on the lower Mississippi River before the river changed course and left the town stranded on an oxbow lake — one of the Mississippi's most dramatic cases of channel migration eliminating a community's reason for existence. At its antebellum peak Rodney had hopes of becoming a major city and even briefly competed for the state capital designation, with churches, commercial buildings, and a prosperous economy tied to the Mississippi cotton trade. Today a small cluster of antebellum brick buildings — including a church with a cannonball embedded in its wall from the Civil War — remains in the isolated Jefferson County woodland. The site's historic buildings and remote landscape make it one of Mississippi's most atmospheric ghost towns.

name: Le Hunt, KS
description: Le Hunt was a small Barton County, Kansas agricultural community in the central Kansas Great Bend Prairie region, one of the modest farming communities that developed during the post-Civil War settlement of the Arkansas River valley of central Kansas. Barton County's county seat of Great Bend and the nearby Arkansas River provided the commercial and transportation focus for the region, leaving smaller communities like Le Hunt without a sustainable economic niche. The central Kansas plains landscape around Le Hunt today is private farmland and rangeland in the heart of Kansas wheat country. Former Kansas farming community sites from the homestead era can yield period coins and agricultural implements with landowner permission.

name: Rush, AR
description: Rush was a small Arkansas mining community in Marion County in the Ozark Mountains along the Buffalo River, established in the 1880s when zinc deposits in the Buffalo River valley attracted miners and their families to this remote Ozark highland. The Rush Mining District produced zinc intermittently through the early twentieth century, with the Morning Star Mine being the principal operation, before declining zinc prices and ore exhaustion ended active mining by the 1940s. Today the Rush ghost town is preserved within the Buffalo National River, the nation's first national river designated in 1972 to protect the free-flowing Buffalo River. The accessible national river site includes several original mining structures and interpretive signage, though metal detecting is prohibited within the national park system.

name: Boston, OH
description: Boston was a small Summit County, Ohio farming community in the Cuyahoga Valley, established in the early nineteenth century as a mill and agricultural service point along the Cuyahoga River that later became part of the Ohio and Erie Canal corridor — one of the most transformative transportation projects in early American history. The mill community at Boston included a general store and tavern serving canal travelers and valley farmers, and the Boston Store — preserved and restored by the National Park Service — survives as one of the finest examples of an 1836 Ohio canal-era general store. The site today is within Cuyahoga Valley National Park, which protects the historic Cuyahoga Valley and the Ohio and Erie Canal Towpath. Metal detecting is prohibited within the national park, but the interpreted historic site and towpath trail make this an excellent destination for Ohio history enthusiasts.

name: Henry River Mill Village, NC
description: Henry River Mill Village was a North Carolina cotton mill community in Burke County, built in 1905 by the Henry family to house workers in their cotton spinning mill in the Catawba River watershed of the Blue Ridge foothills, operating as a self-contained company town with worker housing, a company store, and a church for nearly 80 years until the mill closed in 1987. The remarkably intact collection of mill worker cottages achieved national fame when it was used as a filming location for the fictional District 12 in 'The Hunger Games' (2012), drawing movie fans from across the country to this remote Burke County hollow. Most of the original structures have survived in various states of deterioration, and the site was recently acquired and is being developed as a heritage attraction. The current private ownership status means access and any artifact activity requires explicit permission.

name: Terlingua, TX
description: Terlingua was a significant quicksilver (mercury) mining district and ghost town in Brewster County, Texas in the Big Bend country near the Rio Grande, producing over one-third of the entire United States mercury supply at its World War II peak from the rich cinnabar deposits in the Chisos Mountains region. The Chisos Mining Company dominated Terlingua for decades, employing hundreds of Mexican-American workers in a company town with a theater, store, and housing for several thousand residents. The mine closed in 1946 when lower-cost foreign mercury competed with the dwindling Texas deposits, leaving a remarkable collection of abandoned adobe ruins in the dramatic Big Bend landscape. The area now supports a small artistic and tourism community, and the original ghost town ruins are accessible — one of Texas's most atmospheric and historically significant abandoned places.

name: Nevada City, MT
description: Nevada City was a small Madison County, Montana placer gold mining community along Alder Gulch, established in 1863-1864 in the wake of the great Alder Gulch gold discovery that triggered one of the most spectacular gold rushes in Rocky Mountain history and immediately populated the region with tens of thousands of miners. Nevada City and neighboring Virginia City were part of the same gold camp cluster, and Nevada City briefly had its own post office and commercial district before Virginia City became the dominant center. The site of Nevada City has been restored and expanded as a historical attraction adjacent to Virginia City, with original and relocated historic buildings making it one of Montana's most complete gold rush heritage experiences. The restored village operates seasonally as a tourist attraction, and metal detecting requires permission from the property owners.

name: Bara-Hack, CT
description: Bara-Hack was a small Connecticut colonial farming community in Pomfret, Windham County, established in the late eighteenth century by Welsh settlers who cleared the dense Connecticut forest for farming and operated the settlement through a brief period before the community dispersed. The Bara-Hack name is Welsh for 'breaking of bread,' reflecting the community's cultural origins. The site was abandoned by the mid-nineteenth century and has since acquired a reputation as one of New England's most famously haunted places — allegedly featuring phantom voices, apparitions, and strange phenomena that have drawn paranormal investigators for over a century. The cellar holes, old mill site, and cemetery are on private land in Pomfret, Connecticut, though the site has been historically semi-accessible to visitors.

name: Lax, GA
description: Lax was a small Georgia community — likely in the agricultural interior of the state — that existed as a minor farming settlement or crossroads community in the post-Civil War era Georgia landscape, one of many modest rural communities that developed during the Reconstruction and New South period as Georgia's agricultural economy slowly recovered from the Civil War's devastation. Georgia's interior counties have a rich ante-bellum and Civil War history, and former small community sites in the state's piedmont and coastal plain regions can yield artifacts from multiple historical periods. The exact county location and history of this entry makes detailed description challenging, but the Georgia agricultural landscape retains significant historical character from the antebellum through early twentieth century eras.

name: Burke, ID
description: Burke was a significant silver and lead mining community in Shoshone County, Idaho in the Canyon Creek drainage of the Coeur d'Alene Mining District, one of the world's most productive silver mining districts, where ore was so rich that the canyon was literally too narrow for both the railroad and the creek — the railroad occupied the canyon floor while Canyon Creek flowed underneath it through a series of culverts. The community was subject to violent labor conflict in the 1890s, when mine owners and the Western Federation of Miners fought a series of increasingly violent confrontations culminating in the Bunker Hill explosion of 1899, when miners blew up the Frisco Mill. The canyon narrowness meant buildings were built on multiple levels up the canyon walls, creating a unique architectural character that has been partly preserved. Burke today is largely abandoned, with ruins accessible in the steep canyon above Wallace, Idaho.

name: Buckhorn, IA
description: Buckhorn was a small Iowa community in the rolling agricultural landscape of the state, briefly holding a post office and serving surrounding farm families during Iowa's peak settlement era in the 1870s-1880s before the regional economy consolidated around railroad-connected market towns. Iowa's extraordinarily productive agricultural land supported a dense network of small farming communities in its early settlement decades, but the competitive pressure of better-positioned railroad towns quickly eliminated those without transportation connections. The site today is private farmland in one of America's most productive agricultural states. Former Iowa post office communities from the homestead era can yield period coins and farm implements with landowner permission.

name: Feltville, NJ
description: Feltville was an early New Jersey industrial village in Union County in the Watchung Mountains, built in 1845 by David Felt — a New York City stationery manufacturer — as a complete planned community to house workers in his paper mill and associated industries, becoming one of the most complete surviving examples of a mid-nineteenth century paternalistic industrial village in the northeastern United States. The community was later converted to a resort area called Glenside Park before being acquired by Union County in 1921 and eventually becoming part of the Watchung Reservation. Today the site is preserved within the Watchung Reservation, with several original Greek Revival worker cottages, the mill owner's house, and other structures maintained for historical interpretation. Metal detecting is not permitted within the county reservation.

name: Cuervo, NM
description: Cuervo was a small Guadalupe County, New Mexico ranching and Route 66 community along historic US Route 66 in the eastern New Mexico plains, developing in the early twentieth century as a modest service community for travelers crossing the High Plains of northeastern New Mexico on the Mother Road. The community had a post office, church, and basic services before Interstate 40 bypassed the old highway alignment and eliminated the cross-country traffic that sustained Route 66 communities across New Mexico and Arizona. Today only a handful of crumbling adobe and stone buildings remain at Cuervo, accessible from a frontage road along I-40 in one of the most lonesome stretches of the old route. The sparse and windy Guadalupe County mesa landscape preserves the ruins and is accessible for exploration.

name: Rockerville, SD
description: Rockerville was a Black Hills, South Dakota gold mining community in Pennington County established in 1876 shortly after the great Black Hills gold rush began, thriving briefly as a placer and sluice mining community before the most accessible surface gold was exhausted and miners moved on to deeper operations at Deadwood and Lead. The community had several businesses, a hotel, and a post office during its brief boom years, and a famous hydraulic mining operation that used high-pressure water jets to wash the gold-bearing gravel. Rockerville was later revived in the early twentieth century as a tourist attraction and small community on the road between Rapid City and Custer State Park. The historic core of Rockerville is accessible today, and the surrounding Black Hills National Forest offers open access for gold prospecting in the gulches and streams.

name: Elkmont, TN
description: Elkmont was a small Sevier County, Tennessee community in the Great Smoky Mountains that developed in the early twentieth century as a logging community and subsequently as a summer resort colony, where Knoxville and Chattanooga elite families built summer cottages along Jakes Creek and the Little River beginning in the 1910s-1920s through the Appalachian Club. The Smoky Mountains were heavily logged in the early 1900s, with communities like Elkmont serving as bases for the Little River Lumber Company's operations before the land was acquired for the national park. Today Elkmont's collection of aging summer cottages — some of the oldest structures in Great Smoky Mountains National Park — is the subject of ongoing debate about preservation versus restoration. Metal detecting is prohibited within the national park, but the accessible campground area near the old colony is popular with visitors.

name: Tremont, TN
description: Tremont was a small Sevier County, Tennessee logging and community in the Smoky Mountains area, part of the Little River drainage that was logged extensively by the Little River Lumber Company in the early twentieth century before the land was incorporated into Great Smoky Mountains National Park in the 1930s. The community at Tremont served as a base for logging operations in the upper Little River watershed before the park's establishment required relocating the remaining residents. The Tremont area today is home to the Great Smoky Mountains Institute at Tremont, an environmental education center, accessible via a road through the national park from Townsend, Tennessee. The rich natural and logging heritage of the Tremont area is interpreted through the institute's educational programs.

name: Wahsatch, UT
description: Wahsatch was a small Summit County, Utah community along the Union Pacific Railroad's mainline near the Utah-Wyoming border in the Wasatch Mountains, serving as a railroad section house community and modest service point for travelers and railroad workers crossing the high mountain terrain in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The Echo Canyon approach to the Wasatch was one of the most dramatic sections of the transcontinental railroad, and communities like Wahsatch existed primarily to service the railroad's operations through the mountain terrain. As diesel locomotives replaced steam and railroad technology reduced the need for section house communities, Wahsatch faded from its already modest existence. The surrounding Summit County landscape includes Echo Canyon and the Weber River corridor, with the historic railroad grade still clearly visible in the dramatic canyon terrain.

name: Woodside, UT
description: Woodside was a small railroad and ranching community in Emery County, Utah, established in the early 1900s as a stop along the Denver and Rio Grande Western Railroad in the Price River canyon corridor. The town served as a fuel and water station for steam locomotives and supported a modest cluster of homes, a post office, and a saloon catering to railroad workers and stockmen moving cattle through the region. As diesel locomotives replaced steam engines and the railroad reduced its service stops, Woodside's reason for existing evaporated by the mid-20th century, leaving only scattered debris fields. Metal detectorists may find railroad spikes, bottle glass, and personal items from the worker community on the publicly accessible Bureau of Land Management terrain surrounding the site.

name: Woodrow, UT
description: Woodrow was a small agricultural settlement in Millard County, Utah, founded by Mormon homesteaders in the late 19th century who sought to dry-farm the arid basin lands west of the Sevier River. Thin soils, unpredictable rainfall, and the crushing economics of the Great Depression combined to drive families away by the 1930s, leaving little more than collapsed dugouts and fence lines. Today the area is open BLM rangeland with few visible markers of its settlement history. Careful searching along old field boundaries and around farmstead depressions can turn up hand-wrought farm tools, harness hardware, and homestead-era coins.

name: Winter Quarters, UT
description: Winter Quarters was a coal mining camp in Carbon County, Utah, best remembered as the site of one of the deadliest mine disasters in American history — the May 1, 1900 explosion that killed 200 miners in a single day, making it the worst coal mine accident in U.S. history to that point. The camp supported thousands of miners and their families through the 1880s–1900s as part of the Pleasant Valley coal fields that fueled the Denver and Rio Grande Western Railroad. After the explosion, operations eventually ceased and the population scattered; a cemetery with dozens of victims' graves still survives on the hillside above the old mine portal. The surrounding area around Scofield Reservoir is accessible, and the historic cemetery is maintained — detectorists should respect the burial ground but may explore surrounding mine dump areas for period hardware and mining tokens.

name: Widtsoe, UT
description: Widtsoe was a Mormon farming community founded in the early 1900s in the high valleys of Garfield County, Utah, settled by homesteaders who hoped to dryland-farm the Paunsaugunt Plateau at over 7,000 feet elevation. Persistent drought, severe winters, and poor soil productivity forced most residents to abandon their farms during the 1930s Dust Bowl era, and the community was officially dissolved. A handful of stone foundations and old corrals survive amid the sagebrush flats, and the area is surrounded by Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument lands. The remote location and BLM access make it accessible territory for finds like homestead hardware, cast iron fragments, and 1910s–1930s coins.

name: Westwater, UT
description: Westwater was a tiny railroad watering stop and community in Grand County, Utah, perched along the Colorado River at the Utah-Colorado border where the Denver and Rio Grande Western Railroad skirted the canyon rim. The settlement served railroad workers and a few ranchers during the early 20th century before automated water systems and route changes rendered the stop obsolete. Today the Westwater Canyon area is managed by the Bureau of Land Management as a popular whitewater rafting destination, and the old railroad grade is still visible along the riverside. Detectorists exploring the riparian terraces outside the canyon proper may encounter railroad-era iron hardware, old bottle glass, and camp debris from early rail crews.

name: Wattis, UT
description: Wattis was a coal mining camp in Carbon County, Utah, named after the prominent Wattis brothers whose Utah Construction Company helped build the Western Pacific Railroad through the region in the early 1900s. The town supported a small population of miners and their families who worked the coal seams feeding regional rail demand, peaking during World War I and declining through the 1920s as more accessible mines undercut operations. Virtually nothing above ground remains at the original townsite, which has been reclaimed by the surrounding Helper area landscape. Prospectors working the mine dump spoils piles in nearby public lands may turn up old brass lamp parts, coal company tokens, and hand-forged mine hardware.

name: Watson, UT
description: Watson was a ranching and minor oil camp community in Uintah County, Utah, situated in the Green River basin country north of the Uinta Mountains during the early 20th century. The community served cattlemen and later attracted a brief wave of oil field workers when petroleum prospecting expanded into the Uinta Basin in the 1920s–1940s, but never grew beyond a handful of structures. The area today is remote BLM rangeland with no maintained roads to the original site, making exploration challenging but rewarding for those willing to navigate the terrain. Artifacts from ranch operations — branding irons, saddle hardware, horseshoes — and early oil field equipment fragments are the primary finds.

name: Washakie, UT
description: Washakie was a unique Mormon agricultural colony in Box Elder County, Utah, established in the 1880s by The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints specifically as a settlement for Shoshone converts — one of the few organized Native American farming colonies in Utah history. The community farmed the Cache Valley bottomlands for decades but saw its population dwindle as younger generations moved to urban centers, and the colony was formally dissolved by the mid-20th century. Some structures associated with the community still exist, and the area remains of historical significance to Northwestern Band of the Shoshone Nation members. The cultural sensitivity of the site warrants respectful exploration; surrounding agricultural areas may yield hand-forged farm implements and early 20th-century domestic items.

name: Vipont, UT
description: Vipont was a small lead and silver mining community in Box Elder County, Utah, located in the Grouse Creek Mountains near the Nevada border, active during the late 19th and early 20th centuries as miners worked the mineral-rich ranges of the Raft River district. The isolated camp never grew large due to the remote terrain and modest ore yields, and operations wound down as richer strikes elsewhere drained the labor pool. Today the area is open rangeland with mine shaft collars and tailings piles still visible along the mountain slopes. Metal detectorists can explore the mine dumps for ore specimens and find period hardware, blasting caps fragments, and mining camp relics on the BLM land surrounding the site.

name: Verdure, UT
description: Verdure was a small ranching and farming community in San Juan County, Utah, established by Mormon homesteaders in the 1880s on the benchlands above the Montezuma Creek drainage in the heart of the Four Corners region. The settlement functioned as a loose cluster of family ranches supporting cattle operations across the red rock canyon country, persisting well into the 20th century before dwindling as young people left for larger towns. Scattered stone and adobe foundations remain in the cedar-covered terrain, within reach of Highway 191 south of Monticello. The remote but accessible ranch sites can yield wire-era fence staples, hand-forged tools, horseshoes, and early domestic ceramics for patient searchers.

name: Big Woods, IL
description: Big Woods was a small rural farming settlement in Illinois, typical of the wave of agricultural communities that sprang up across the Midwest during the mid-19th century as settlers pushed into the rich prairie and timber border country. The community likely supported a sawmill, a general store, and a church before consolidation of farms, improved road networks, and the pull of larger nearby towns left it economically stranded. No substantial physical remnants survive above ground, and the site has been absorbed into surrounding agricultural land. Hobbyist detectorists working the old farmstead corners and fence lines in the area may encounter mid-to-late 1800s farm implements, ceramics, and domestic coins.

name: Goeselville, IL
description: Goeselville was a small agricultural community in Illinois, one of dozens of hopeful farming villages platted during the land boom of the 1830s and 1840s that never achieved the critical mass needed for long-term survival. The town likely had a brief window of activity with a tavern or store before a passing railroad to a neighboring community sealed its fate by drawing commerce away. The original plat has long since been folded into surrounding farmland, leaving only old survey records as evidence of its existence. Metal detectorists working the section corners near the historic plat site may recover early American copper coins, cut nails, and domestic hardware.

name: Ritchie, IL
description: Ritchie was a small rural settlement in Illinois, platted during the expansive settlement era of the mid-1800s when speculators and community-builders laid out dozens of paper towns across the fertile Illinois prairie. Like many such communities, it failed to attract a railroad connection and gradually lost its population to more prosperous neighboring towns over the latter half of the 19th century. The site is now absorbed into agricultural fields with no surviving structures. Searching old homestead depressions and fence rows in the surrounding farmland may yield early American coins, harness hardware, and hand-forged iron implements.

name: Wallingford, IL
description: Wallingford was a modest farming community in Illinois, established by Yankee settlers from New England in the mid-1800s who brought with them a strong tradition of town planning and civic institutions including a school and church. The community struggled to compete as regional trade routes bypassed it in favor of railroad towns that could offer faster goods movement, and its population gradually drained away by the late 19th century. No above-ground structures remain at the original townsite. The old church lot and school grounds, identifiable through historic maps, are worth investigating for 19th-century buttons, coins, and domestic fragments.

name: Blom, MI
description: Blom was a small community in Michigan's Upper Peninsula or northern Lower Peninsula, likely a logging camp or small farming settlement established during the great Midwestern timber rush of the 1870s–1890s when vast tracts of white pine were clear-cut to feed the building boom in Chicago and the industrial Midwest. When the timber played out, lumbering camps were abandoned almost overnight, leaving only mill pond earthworks and scattered debris. Today the site is surrounded by second-growth forest managed by the state or federal government. Logging-era artifacts — cant hook heads, peavy spikes, ox shoes, and camp cookware — are the primary detectable relics from these sites.

name: Nathan, MI
description: Nathan was a small rural settlement in Michigan, established during the agricultural or early industrial era by settlers seeking to farm or log the region's resources. Like many small Michigan outposts, the community dwindled as young residents left for industrial cities like Detroit, Flint, and Grand Rapids during the early 20th century, leaving behind only foundations and overgrown lots. The site is now surrounded by state or county land with public access in most areas. Searches around old cellar holes and farm perimeters may turn up late 1800s–early 1900s coins, farm implements, and homestead ceramics.

name: Candelaria, NV
description: Candelaria was a boisterous silver mining camp in Mineral County, Nevada, perched in the Candelaria Hills east of Hawthorne, booming in the 1870s and 1880s as rich silver ore poured from the Mount Diablo district mines and supporting a population of over 1,500 at its peak. The camp had a Wells Fargo office, several saloons, a newspaper, and all the trappings of a functioning Nevada silver town before declining ore grades and falling silver prices collapsed the economy by the early 1890s. Today the site retains scattered stone foundation ruins and old mine workings visible across the open desert landscape, accessible via dirt roads from Highway 95. The desert setting preserves metal artifacts well — miners' checks, tools, bottle glass, and personal items are common finds in the mine dumps and around the old townsite.

name: Obed, IL
description: Obed was a small agricultural settlement in Illinois, likely a religious or community-organized farming colony established by Midwestern settlers during the great Illinois land rush of the 1830s–1850s. The settlement never achieved the population density needed to sustain commerce and gradually collapsed as residents moved to more connected townships with railroad access. Only old county plat maps indicate where the community stood, with no surviving above-ground evidence. Detectorists searching old road traces and farmstead perimeters near the historic plat may find pre–Civil War American coinage, hand-forged nails, and domestic hardware typical of early Illinois frontier settlements.

name: Pleak, IL
description: Pleak was a small settlement in Illinois, one of the many short-lived community experiments of the mid-19th century when optimistic settlers platted town after town on the Illinois prairie hoping to attract a post office, a store, or a depot. Without a railroad connection, most such communities withered within a generation as their populations consolidated into larger nearby centers. The site is now agricultural land with no surviving structures. Farm field edges and historic road traces are the most productive areas for detectorists seeking early American coins, harness fittings, and frontier-era domestic artifacts.

name: Great Isaac Cay, Bahamas
description: Great Isaac Cay is a small, isolated island in the Bahamas located at the northern edge of the Great Bahama Bank, historically notable for its lighthouse established in 1855 to warn ships navigating the treacherous Northwest Providence Channel. A small community of lighthouse keepers and their families maintained a permanent presence on the island for over a century, but Hurricane Andrew's devastation in 1992 prompted the Bahamas government to automate the lighthouse and withdraw all residents. Today the cay is uninhabited, with the automated lighthouse still functioning and the ruins of keepers' cottages slowly being reclaimed by tropical vegetation.
