The island is occasionally visited by private boaters, and the ruins offer a rare opportunity to find British colonial-era hardware, lighthouse-keeper domestic items, and early 20th-century artifacts in a remote Caribbean setting.

name: Concrete, CO
description: Concrete was a small quarrying and ranching community in Gunnison County, Colorado, situated in the upper Tomichi Valley where limestone deposits supported a brief industrial operation in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The community served the workers who extracted and processed stone from the surrounding formations, supplemented by ranching families using the high-altitude grasslands. Operations wound down as the market for locally quarried stone declined with the availability of cheaper Portland cement, and the community faded away. The remote valley location off Tomichi Creek Road offers open public land access where detectorists may find quarry equipment fragments, early wire-era ranch hardware, and 1890s–1920s coins.

name: Mantua, AL
description: Mantua was a small agricultural village in Etowah County, Alabama, established by antebellum settlers along one of the creek drainages feeding the Coosa River system during the 1830s–1850s cotton era. The community supported a rural church, a school, and small cotton-related trade, but never attained town status before the Civil War and Reconstruction disrupted the regional economy. Over the following decades, improved roads and later automobile culture drew residents toward Gadsden and other Etowah County centers, leaving Mantua to disappear into the surrounding farmland. The old church yard and community gathering sites are worth exploring for antebellum coins, military buttons, and early domestic hardware.

name: Arcadia, MS
description: Arcadia was a small Mississippi farming community, likely situated in the rolling hills of the state's central or northern region, established in the antebellum era by plantation-economy settlers growing cotton on the rich loessial soils. The town served as a minor local trading center with a general store, a gin, and a church before the collapse of the plantation economy after the Civil War and successive agricultural downturns in the late 19th and early 20th centuries gradually emptied it. Only old county records and a possible cemetery mark where Arcadia once stood. Metal detectorists working old road traces and farmstead perimeters in the area may find pre–Civil War American coins, Confederate buttons, and plantation-era domestic items.

name: Arnot, MS
description: Arnot was a rural community in Mississippi, typical of the dozens of small farming settlements established across the state during the antebellum cotton boom of the 1830s–1860s that lacked the transportation infrastructure needed for long-term survival. The community likely had a church, a school, and a general store at its peak, serving the surrounding plantation families and small landholders, before economic hardships following the Civil War and the boll weevil crisis of the early 20th century depleted its population. Today the site is farmland or forested with no surviving structures. Searching along old road traces and around historic church or school sites may yield antebellum copper coins, military relics, and domestic fragments.

name: Artonish, MS
description: Artonish was a Mississippi plantation community in the Natchez District of Jefferson County, built around a grand antebellum cotton plantation whose main house and outbuildings formed the nucleus of a small community of enslaved workers, overseers, and family members. Artonish Plantation was one of the more prosperous estates in the Natchez region during the 1840s–1860s, but the Civil War and emancipation shattered the plantation economy, and the surrounding community dissolved as freedpeople and ruined planters both moved on. The Artonish area today is largely forested private land, though the old plantation road trace and some structural ruins may survive. Antebellum domestic artifacts, plantation tokens, and Civil War military items are historically significant finds from this area.

name: Baleshed, MS
description: Baleshed was a small rural Mississippi community, one of the countless farm-era settlements that dotted the state's agricultural landscape during the 19th century and vanished as rural consolidation and economic hardship thinned the population across multiple generations. The settlement likely supported a church and a small store serving nearby farming families growing cotton or corn on the region's soils. No notable physical remains survive above ground, and the land has been absorbed into modern agricultural or timber operations. Detectorists working permission-obtained farmland in the area may find 19th-century domestic items, agricultural hardware, and early American coins.

name: Bankston, MS
description: Bankston was a small community in Mississippi, established in the antebellum era as a minor rural trading center serving the farming families of the surrounding region. The community had a store, church, and perhaps a school that served a modest cluster of households before the double blow of the post–Civil War depression and the late 19th-century agricultural crisis steadily erased its commercial life. Today Bankston exists only in county records and on historic maps, with no surviving commercial structures. The old community sites are potentially productive for 19th-century copper and silver coinage, military relics from the Civil War era, and domestic household items.

name: Beatties Bluff, MS
description: Beatties Bluff was a Mississippi River bluff-top community in Warren County, Mississippi, likely a pre–Civil War landing and trading site that served steamboat traffic on the great river during the antebellum era. Like many river landings, it combined agricultural trade with ferry operations and a tavern or store, bustling during the cotton-shipping season before steamboating declined in the post–Civil War era. The high Mississippi River bluffs of Warren County have eroded significantly over the decades, and much of the original landing site may have been lost to the river. Accessible bluff-top areas can yield steamboat-era hardware, antebellum coins, and mid-19th century domestic artifacts.

name: Ben Lomond, MS
description: Ben Lomond was a small Mississippi community, likely named by Scots-Irish settlers in the antebellum era who built a church, a school, and a few commercial establishments to serve the surrounding farming region. The community persisted through the Civil War and Reconstruction but declined in the late 19th century as improved transportation bypassed it in favor of railroad towns that could offer regular freight service. Today the community name survives only in local historical memory and old county maps. The surrounding farmland, particularly around old church sites and road intersections, may yield antebellum and Civil War era coins, buttons, and domestic fragments for detectorists working with landowner permission.

name: Pearce, MS
description: Pearce was a small Mississippi farming community that served as a minor local center for rural households growing cotton across the rolling hills of the state's interior during the 19th century. The settlement likely had a church and a country store before declining in the early 20th century as automobiles and improved roads allowed farmers to patronize larger nearby towns. No physical structures survive above ground, and the site has been absorbed into surrounding agricultural operations. Detectorists with landowner permission may explore the old community site for 19th-century domestic items, Civil War era relics, and early American coins.

name: Liverpool, MS
description: Liverpool was a minor Mississippi landing and farming community, likely situated on or near a river or creek where early settlers could ship cotton by flatboat or steamboat to downstream markets during the antebellum era. The name suggests it was platted by ambitious promoters hoping to create a commercial hub modeled on the great English port city, but the reality was a modest cluster of homes, a landing, and a tavern or store. The community faded after the Civil War disrupted the regional cotton economy and steamboat traffic declined in favor of the railroad. Detectorists exploring the old landing sites along Mississippi waterways may find antebellum coins, steamboat hardware, and Civil War-era military relics.

name: Plumville, MS
description: Plumville was a small rural community in Mississippi, established during the antebellum settlement era and named for the wild plum thickets common throughout the region's bottomlands and creek margins. The town served as a minor local center with a church and store before the post–Civil War economic depression and subsequent decades of agricultural hardship steadily drained its population. Today the community survives only in historical records, with no physical structures remaining above ground. Searches around old road traces and church sites may yield 19th-century domestic ceramics, coins, and farm hardware for detectorists working with landowner permission.

name: Oswego, MS
description: Oswego was a Mississippi farming community established in the mid-19th century, one of many settlements platted with optimistic names borrowed from prosperous Northern cities in hopes of attracting settlers and investment to the Deep South frontier. The town functioned as a minor agricultural trade center, handling cotton and corn from surrounding farms, before economic decline following the Civil War and Reconstruction era reduced it to a mere crossroads. No commercial structures survive, and the site is now agricultural land. Old fence lines, road traces, and overgrown lots in the Oswego area can yield antebellum coins, Civil War relics, and 19th-century domestic artifacts for patient searchers.

name: Coars Springs, MS
description: Coars Springs was a small rural community in Mississippi, likely built around a mineral or freshwater spring that served as a local gathering point for farming families in the surrounding area during the 19th century. Springs were important social and commercial anchors on the frontier, often hosting a tavern, a store, and eventually a post office, before the advantages faded with improved road access to larger towns. The spring and any associated structures have long since been absorbed into private farmland or forest. The old spring site and surrounding homestead areas may preserve domestic artifacts, 19th-century coins, and early iron implements for detectorists with landowner access.

name: Westville, Simpson County, MS
description: Westville in Simpson County was a small Mississippi agricultural community, separate from the better-known Westville in Holmes County, established during the mid-19th century to serve the farming families of central Mississippi's pine belt region. The town had a church, a store, and a post office at its height before consolidation of rural communities under improved transportation gradually drew its population toward larger centers like Mendenhall. No physical evidence of the original community survives above ground today. The surrounding Simpson County countryside, worked with landowner permission, may yield 19th-century domestic coins, farm hardware, and Civil War-era relics.

name: Mount Carmel, MS
description: Mount Carmel was a Mississippi farming community established in the early-to-mid 19th century, likely organized around a church of that name as was common practice in the Scots-Irish and Baptist-dominated rural South. The community served surrounding plantation and small-farm families with a church, a school, and possibly a general store, flourishing briefly before the Civil War and post-war agricultural depression reduced it to a ghost. Today the Mount Carmel church cemetery may be the only surviving physical evidence of the community, surrounded by private farmland. Detectorists with permission may explore around the old church grounds for antebellum coins, Civil War buttons, and domestic hardware from the active era.

name: Logtown, MS
description: Logtown was a once-thriving lumber and naval stores community in Hancock County, Mississippi, situated in the vast longleaf pine forests near the Pearl River estuary where timber was processed and shipped to markets across the Gulf Coast from the mid-1800s through the early 1900s. The town supported sawmill workers, turpentine camp laborers, and their families, with a school, church, and company store at its height before the combination of clear-cutting and Hurricane Camille's 1969 devastation effectively destroyed what remained of the community. Today Logtown is part of the buffer zone of NASA's Stennis Space Center, making it a federally controlled area off-limits to the general public. The site holds considerable historical significance as a lost Gulf South lumber town, though access requires NASA authorization.

name: Gainesville, MS
description: Gainesville was an antebellum-era Mississippi community, once the county seat of Hancock County before the seat was moved to Bay St. Louis, situated along the lower Pearl River where cotton planting and early commerce created a brief regional center in the 1820s–1830s. The town had a courthouse, a tavern, and several commercial establishments during its peak years, but the county seat designation was lost to better-situated competitors and the community declined through the Civil War era. Today the Gainesville area is heavily forested and private, with a historic cemetery surviving as the most tangible reminder of the former community. Civil War relics, antebellum coins, and early 19th-century domestic artifacts are potential finds for detectorists with landowner permission.

name: Pink, MS
description: Pink was a small Mississippi farming community, likely named after an early settler family or the wild phlox that bloomed along its creek drainages, established during the mid-to-late 19th century as a minor rural trading post serving the surrounding hill country farmsteads. The community may have had a sawmill and cotton gin in addition to a general store and church, capitalizing on the pine timber and cotton economy of the Mississippi interior. As rural depopulation accelerated through the early 20th century, Pink faded from the landscape without leaving notable physical traces. Farm field edges and old road intersections near the community location may yield 19th-century coins, agricultural hardware, and domestic artifacts for detectorists.

name: Riverside, Lafayette County, MS
description: Riverside in Lafayette County was a Mississippi farming community near the Tallahatchie River or one of its tributaries, established by antebellum settlers who valued the water access for cotton shipping and mill operations during the 1840s–1860s. The community served as a minor trade center for surrounding plantation and small-farm families before the post–Civil War economic collapse and subsequent decades of agricultural hardship eroded its population. Today the original community site is private farmland or bottomland hardwood forest. Riverbank areas near the old community site may yield antebellum coins, steamboat hardware fragments, and farm implements for patient detectorists with landowner permission.

name: Cotton Gin Port, MS
description: Cotton Gin Port was a significant antebellum commercial settlement in Monroe County, Mississippi, situated on the Tombigbee River where a cotton gin and river landing served as a major shipping hub for the surrounding plantation district during the 1820s–1840s. At its peak the community had a tavern, warehouses, a post office, and a thriving trade in ginned cotton baled for shipment down the Tombigbee to Mobile, making it one of the more commercially active interior landings in northeast Mississippi. The community declined sharply after the Mobile and Ohio Railroad bypassed it in favor of Aberdeen and other settlements in the 1850s, gutting the river trade economy. Today Cotton Gin Port is a historical site with some archaeological investigations conducted in the area, and the Tombigbee riverbanks may yield early 19th-century commercial artifacts and antebellum domestic items.

name: Electric Mills, MS
description: Electric Mills was an early 20th-century sawmill and company town in Kemper County, Mississippi, built around one of the region's innovative electrically powered timber mills that processed the vast longleaf pine forests of east-central Mississippi during the 1910s–1930s. The company town included worker housing, a commissary, a school, and a church, all owned and operated by the lumber company, and it functioned as a self-contained settlement for hundreds of mill workers and their families. When the surrounding timberland was exhausted by the mid-20th century, the mill closed and the company town dissolved, leaving behind only foundations and the community's name on county maps. Searches around old mill sites and company town footprints can yield early 20th-century coins, industrial hardware, and domestic items from the mill worker community.

name: Woolworth, MS
description: Woolworth was a small Mississippi farming community named for an early settler family, established in the rural agricultural districts of the state during the mid-to-late 19th century and serving as a minor trade point for cotton farmers in the surrounding area. The community had a post office for a period and a church or small store before rural depopulation through the 20th century reduced it to a name on old postal records. Today no physical structures remain, and the site is private farmland. Detectorists with landowner permission may find 19th-century domestic artifacts, Civil War-era relics, and agricultural hardware in the vicinity of the old community.

name: Rocky Springs, MS
description: Rocky Springs was a prosperous antebellum farming community in Claiborne County, Mississippi along the Old Natchez Trace, booming in the 1820s–1830s when cotton planting made it one of the wealthiest settlements in the state's interior before yellow fever epidemics, soil depletion, and the Civil War systematically destroyed the community over the following decades. At its peak, Rocky Springs had over 2,500 residents, multiple churches, a post office, and a thriving commercial district — an astonishing scale for such a remote inland location. Today Rocky Springs is preserved as part of Natchez Trace Parkway, managed by the National Park Service, and visitors can walk to the surviving 1837 brick Methodist church and the community cemetery. Access for metal detecting is prohibited within the national parkway, but the area's rich history draws historians and enthusiasts who can explore publicly accessible adjacent lands.

name: Uniontown, MS
description: Uniontown was a small Mississippi farming community, likely established in the antebellum era and named by optimistic settlers seeking to evoke national unity in a landscape deeply divided by sectional tensions over slavery. The community served the surrounding cotton-farming households with a church, school, and store, but never grew large enough to survive the economic shocks of the Civil War and Reconstruction period. Today the community name appears on old county maps and historical records, with no surviving physical structures. Old church sites and road traces near the historic community location are potential areas for finding antebellum coins, Civil War military relics, and 19th-century domestic artifacts.

name: Dogtown, MS
description: Dogtown was an informal rural settlement in Mississippi, typical of the colloquially named crossroads communities scattered throughout the Deep South that served as gathering points for farming families living beyond the reach of formal town amenities. The community may have had a blacksmith shop, a gristmill, or a tavern alongside its informal social functions, peaking in the mid-to-late 19th century before improved transportation drew commerce to larger centers. No formal historical records document the community in detail, and the site has been absorbed into surrounding private agricultural land. Old crossroads and fence line intersections in the vicinity may yield 19th-century coins, farm hardware, and domestic items.

name: Orwood, MS
description: Orwood was a small Mississippi farming community in Carroll County, established during the antebellum era as a minor local trading center for the surrounding cotton-growing plantation district. The community had a church and possibly a small store, surviving through the Civil War and Reconstruction before the steady rural depopulation of the late 19th and early 20th centuries emptied it. Today Orwood exists only as a name on historic maps, and the site is private agricultural land. The Carroll County countryside in the Orwood area, explored with landowner permission, may yield antebellum domestic artifacts, Civil War-era military items, and 19th-century farm implements.

name: Old Town, Calhoun County, MS
description: Old Town in Calhoun County was a Mississippi community that predated the formal organization of surrounding settlements, established by early Choctaw-era traders and pioneer settlers in the 1820s–1830s before the removal of the Choctaw Nation opened the region to large-scale American settlement. The community served as an early trading post and gathering point for both Native American and settler populations during the transitional decade before the county was formally organized and newer, better-located towns absorbed the trade. Almost nothing survives above ground at the old townsite, which is now forested or farmed private land. The pre–1840 era of this location makes it a particularly interesting target for searchers, who might find early American trade goods, Native American items, and pioneer-era coins with landowner permission.

name: Pontocola, MS
description: Pontocola was a small rural community in Mississippi, established during the antebellum settlement era and serving the farming families of the surrounding region with church services, a small store, and perhaps a post office at its peak. The community name reflects the region's Choctaw linguistic heritage — many Mississippi place names derive from the Choctaw language — and likely occupied land previously used by Native American communities before the forced removal of the 1830s. Today the community survives only in historical records and old maps, having been absorbed into the surrounding agricultural landscape. Detectorists with landowner permission may find trade era artifacts, antebellum coins, and early domestic hardware at old community sites.

name: Palo Alto, MS
description: Palo Alto was a small Mississippi farming community, likely named in the patriotic fervor following the 1846 Battle of Palo Alto during the Mexican-American War — a naming convention common across the South and Midwest during that era. The community served as a minor rural center for surrounding plantation and farm families growing cotton in the region, with a church and store at its height before post–Civil War economic decline reduced it to a crossroads and eventually nothing at all. Today Palo Alto exists only on old county plat maps and postal records. The surrounding rural landscape, explored with landowner permission, may yield mid-19th-century coins, domestic artifacts, and farm implements from the community's active years.

name: Pigeon Roost, MS
description: Pigeon Roost was a Mississippi farming community, named for the enormous wild passenger pigeon roosts that once darkened the skies over the forested Southeast — a phenomenon that disappeared entirely with the species' extinction by 1914. The settlement functioned as a minor rural center serving surrounding farm families in the mid-to-late 19th century, with a church and community gathering space at its core. Today the community is gone, along with the passenger pigeons that gave it its name, leaving only old county records. The area around former Pigeon Roost community sites can yield 19th-century farm implements, domestic items, and early American coins for detectorists working with landowner permission.

name: Riverside, Forrest County, MS
description: Riverside in Forrest County was a small Mississippi community along one of the region's creek or river drainages, established in the late 19th or early 20th century to serve the farming and timber interests developing the longleaf pine lands of south-central Mississippi. The community had limited commercial infrastructure, likely a sawmill-era company store or a small church, before timber exhaustion and rural consolidation emptied it by the mid-20th century. Today the site is forested or agricultural private land in Forrest County near Hattiesburg. Searches around old sawmill sites and homestead perimeters in the area may yield early 20th-century industrial hardware, company-store tokens, and domestic artifacts.

name: Wilson, IL
description: Wilson was a small rural community in Illinois, established during the great prairie settlement era of the 1830s–1860s by Yankee and Southern-origin farmers who saw opportunity in the state's rich agricultural lands. The community functioned as a minor rural crossroads with a church, school, and country store for surrounding farming families before the arrival of better transportation to neighboring towns drained its commercial life by the late 19th century. Today Wilson is absorbed into the surrounding Illinois agricultural landscape with no surviving structures. Old church lots and road intersections near the historic community plat are worth searching for early American coins, farm hardware, and domestic artifacts.

name: Benjaminville, IL
description: Benjaminville was a small Illinois farming community platted in the early settlement era of the 1830s–1840s, likely by a founder named Benjamin who hoped to anchor a commercial center to serve the surrounding prairie homesteaders. Like hundreds of similar Illinois paper towns, it failed to attract the railroad connection needed for long-term commercial viability and gradually faded as residents patronized better-situated nearby communities. No physical evidence of the original plat survives above ground, and the site is agricultural land. The old plat location, traceable through historic maps, may yield pre–Civil War coins, cut nails, and domestic hardware for detectorists with landowner permission.

name: Bethel, IL
description: Bethel was a community in Illinois organized around a religious congregation, a pattern common throughout the evangelical Protestant frontier South and Midwest where Baptist, Methodist, and Presbyterian churches often served as the anchor for surrounding settlements. The Bethel church likely served as a school and community hall as well, drawing surrounding farm families for worship, education, and commerce, before population shifts and improved roads drew the community's residents toward larger towns with more services. A cemetery associated with the Bethel church may be the only surviving physical evidence of the community today. Detectorists should respect any associated burial grounds while exploring adjacent areas for 19th-century coins and domestic hardware.

name: Totten Prairie, IL
description: Totten Prairie was a small Illinois agricultural community named for the open grassland landscape that characterized the settlement when pioneers first arrived in the 1830s–1840s from the forested East to find vast treeless prairies perfect for breaking into farmland. The community served surrounding homesteaders with a church and school, functioning as a social gathering point during the decades when surrounding land was being broken and fenced. As the prairie was fully settled and farms were consolidated, the need for a local community center diminished and Totten Prairie was absorbed into the larger township structure. Old farmstead locations on the historic prairie can yield early American coinage, plow iron, and domestic artifacts.

name: Civer, IL
description: Civer was a small rural community in Illinois, one of the many short-lived settlements platted during the explosive growth era of the 1830s–1860s when land speculators and community organizers laid out town after town across the Illinois prairie in hopes of capturing railroad traffic or agricultural trade. The community never achieved a railroad connection or post office longevity, and its population dispersed to larger and better-connected neighboring towns within a generation. Today Civer's location is traceable only through historic county maps and plat records. The old townsite area, explored with landowner permission, may contain 19th-century domestic artifacts, pre–Civil War coins, and farm implements.

name: Sugarville, Il
description: Sugarville was a small Illinois agricultural community, named either for early sugar maple groves in the area or for a sugar camp or refinery operation that briefly processed beet or cane products in the settlement era. The community served surrounding farming families as a minor local center with a church and store, peaking in the mid-to-late 19th century before rural consolidation and improved transportation drew its residents to larger nearby towns. No substantial physical remains survive above ground. Searches around old farmstead sites and historic road traces near Sugarville's documented location may yield 19th-century farm hardware, domestic items, and early American coinage.

name: Bybee, IL
description: Bybee was a small Illinois farming community established during the mid-19th century settlement period by farm families seeking to claim the rich black soils of the Illinois prairie for agriculture. The community functioned as a rural gathering point with a church, a one-room school, and perhaps a blacksmith shop serving the surrounding homestead farms. As the prairie was fully converted to agriculture and rural populations consolidated in larger towns through the late 19th and early 20th centuries, Bybee faded from the landscape. The area around old farm sites near the historic Bybee community location can turn up 19th-century coins, harness hardware, and domestic artifacts for patient detectorists.

name: Coltonville, IL
description: Coltonville was a small Illinois farming community, platted during the optimistic settlement era of the 1840s–1860s and named for an early settler or landowner who staked out a town site on the Illinois prairie. The community functioned as a minor rural commercial center for surrounding farm families, with a church and store at its core, before failing to secure the railroad connection needed to sustain long-term commercial viability. No physical evidence of the original townsite survives above ground today. The old plat location, identified through historic county maps, may yield pre–Civil War domestic artifacts, early American coins, and cast iron hardware for detectorists with landowner access.

name: Brush Point, IL
description: Brush Point was a small Illinois community situated on or near a river or lake peninsula characterized by dense brush or timber — a common descriptive place name throughout the Midwest that reflected the landscape settlers encountered when they first arrived. The community likely served river travelers and nearby farmers in the mid-19th century with a tavern, a ferry crossing, or a minor trading post before improved roads and bridges made such geographic landmarks less commercially significant. Today the site has been absorbed into surrounding agricultural or riparian land. Old river landing areas and farmstead perimeters near the original Brush Point location may yield 19th-century coins, domestic items, and watercraft hardware.

name: Bourbonais, IL
description: Bourbonais was an early French-Canadian settlement in Kankakee County, Illinois, one of the oldest European-origin communities in northeastern Illinois, established by French-Canadian fur traders and settlers in the 1830s near a ford on the Kankakee River where the community of Bourbonnais (the modern spelling) eventually grew into a permanent village. The original settlement area preceded the formal town plat and served as a gathering point for the mixed French-Canadian and Potawatomi population of the region before American settlers arrived in force. Today the village of Bourbonnais is an active community, but the ghost-town designation likely refers to the original pre-plat settlement area that predated the formal incorporation. Historic French-Canadian domestic artifacts, fur-trade-era items, and early American coins are potential finds from the oldest occupation layers.

name: Daggett, IL
description: Daggett was a small Illinois farming community, established during the mid-19th century settlement of the Illinois prairie by families seeking the rich agricultural land available through the government land sale system. The community had a church and perhaps a school, functioning as a local social center for surrounding homesteaders before declining in the latter half of the 19th century as railroad-served towns attracted the regional commerce. No physical structures remain today, and the original community site is agricultural land. Farm field margins and old road traces near the historic Daggett community location may yield 19th-century coins, agricultural hardware, and domestic fragments.

name: Reeds Crossing, IL
description: Reeds Crossing was a small Illinois community centered on a river or creek ford where an early settler named Reed established a crossing and perhaps a tavern or ferry service for travelers moving through the region during the mid-19th century. Such crossing points were natural gathering spots for trade and socialization during the era before reliable bridges, and they often developed into small communities with a blacksmith and a store alongside the crossing operation. As bridges replaced fords and road alignments shifted, Reeds Crossing lost its commercial reason for existing and faded from the landscape. Old creek and river crossing sites in the area may preserve 19th-century coins, travel-related hardware, and domestic items from the tavern era.

name: Cardiff, IL
description: Cardiff was a small Illinois community platted during the boom era of Illinois settlement, likely named by Welsh immigrant settlers who brought the name of their homeland's capital to the Midwest prairie where they hoped to establish a prosperous new community. The town may have had a brief commercial existence with a store and church before failing to attract a railroad depot, which proved fatal to scores of similar Illinois communities in the 1850s–1870s. Today Cardiff exists only in old county plat records. The historic community location, accessed with landowner permission, may yield mid-19th-century domestic artifacts, Welsh-community items, and early American coinage.

name: Tracy, IL
description: Tracy was a small rural community in Illinois, established in the mid-to-late 19th century and named for an early landowner or settler family that dominated the local landscape. The community served surrounding farm families with a church, school, and country store before demographic consolidation and improved transportation drew its residents toward larger neighboring towns through the early 20th century. No physical structures survive at the original community site. Old farmstead perimeters and road traces near the historic Tracy community location may yield late 19th-century coins, agricultural hardware, and domestic artifacts.

name: Houstonville, IL
description: Houstonville was a small Illinois farming community, platted during the mid-19th century and named for a prominent local settler or landowner who helped establish the settlement in the rich agricultural country of central or southern Illinois. The community had a church and a country store at its height, serving as a minor rural center before railroad construction to nearby towns effectively bypassed Houstonville and drew its commercial life away. Today the original townsite is agricultural land with no surviving structures. Old church lots and road intersections identified through historic plat maps may be productive areas for 19th-century finds.

name: Kumler, IL
description: Kumler was a small Illinois community, likely named for a German or Swiss-German immigrant family — the Kumler surname being common among German-speaking Pennsylvania Dutch settlers who spread across Ohio and Illinois in the early 19th century. The community served as a minor agricultural trading point for surrounding farm families, possibly with a German Lutheran or Reformed church at its core, before disappearing from active use by the late 19th or early 20th century. No physical evidence of the original settlement survives above ground. Old church sites and farmstead perimeters near the Kumler community location may yield German-American domestic artifacts, 19th-century coins, and agricultural hardware.

name: Lone Tree Corners, IL
description: Lone Tree Corners was a small Illinois community named for a solitary tree — likely a large bur oak or cottonwood — that served as a landmark at a rural crossroads, a common naming convention for Midwestern communities that grew up around distinctive natural features visible across the flat prairie landscape. The community may have had a blacksmith shop, a school, and a store serving surrounding farm families in the mid-to-late 19th century before better-connected neighboring towns absorbed its commerce. No structures remain today. Old crossroads sites and farmstead areas near the historic community location may yield 19th-century coins, agricultural implements, and domestic hardware.

name: Chamness, IL
description: Chamness was a small Illinois farming community, platted during the mid-19th century and named for the Chamness family — a surname found among Quaker settlers from North Carolina and Indiana who moved into central Illinois during the antebellum era. The community served as a minor local gathering point for surrounding Quaker and mixed-denomination farm families, with a Friends meeting house or other church at its core. As the rural Quaker population consolidated and moved to larger centers through the late 19th century, Chamness faded from the landscape. The old meeting grounds and farmstead perimeters in the area may yield 19th-century domestic artifacts and early American coins.

name: Halfway, IL (Little Juarez)
description: Halfway (Little Juarez) was an informal Illinois settlement that earned its colorful dual name from its position midway between two larger communities and its reputation as a rough-edged roadhouse stop notorious enough to earn the unofficial Mexican border-town nickname. The community thrived as a wayside tavern and gathering point during an era when roadside commerce was less regulated, drawing travelers, farmhands, and locals seeking entertainment beyond the reach of more respectable neighboring towns. As highway patterns shifted and prohibition-era crackdowns changed the character of roadside commerce, the settlement faded from even informal existence. The old roadhouse site, now agricultural or wooded land, may preserve early 20th-century bottle glass, domestic items, and coins from its active commercial years.

name: Halfway, IL
description: Halfway, Illinois — distinct from the Little Juarez-nicknamed version — was a small farming community occupying a geographic midpoint between two larger centers, a common naming convention for communities that served as waypoints along early roads and toll routes through the prairie. The community likely had a tavern, a store, and a blacksmith at its height during the mid-19th century when road travel demanded regular stops for horses and travelers. As improved roads and eventually the automobile made such midpoint stops obsolete, Halfway faded from commercial significance. Old road trace areas and historic farmstead sites near the documented Halfway location may yield 19th-century travel artifacts, coins, and agricultural hardware.

name: White Oak Springs, IL
description: White Oak Springs was an Illinois community established around a mineral or freshwater spring beneath a notable white oak grove, a type of geographic anchor common in the rolling timberland-to-prairie transition zones of central Illinois during the mid-19th century. Springs in the Illinois landscape provided reliable water for livestock and travelers, often spawning taverns and small resort operations before the era of piped water. The community likely had a small hotel or bathhouse, a church, and a store before the spring lost its commercial significance and the population dispersed. The old spring site and surrounding grounds may preserve 19th-century domestic artifacts, bottle glass from the resort era, and coins.

name: Crawfordsville, IL
description: Crawfordsville was a small Illinois farming community, likely named for a founder or early landowner in the tradition of the dozens of Illinois towns honoring prominent settler families in their names. The community served surrounding agricultural interests with a church, school, and store during the mid-to-late 19th century before being eclipsed by better-connected neighboring towns with railroad access. Today the community site is agricultural land with no surviving structures. The historic community location, traceable through old Illinois plat maps, may yield 19th-century domestic artifacts, early American coins, and farm implements.

name: Dutton, IL
description: Dutton was a small rural community in Illinois, established during the prairie settlement era of the mid-19th century and named for an early settler family or a local landowner who helped plat the original town site. The community served surrounding farming families as a minor local center with a church and perhaps a country store before demographic and economic consolidation drew its residents toward larger towns with better services. No physical evidence of the original community survives today. The historic Dutton community location, identified through old county maps, may yield 19th-century coins, agricultural hardware, and domestic items for detectorists with landowner permission.

name: Elm Point, IL
description: Elm Point was a small Illinois community named for a point of land on a river or lake characterized by a notable stand of American elm trees, a tree species that once dominated the Illinois riparian landscape before Dutch elm disease devastated the population in the mid-20th century. The community likely served river travelers and local farmers as a minor landing or crossroads community during the mid-19th century. As the elms died, the rivers were engineered, and the roads shifted, the geographic anchor that gave Elm Point its identity disappeared along with the community itself. Old river landing and farmstead areas near the historic Elm Point location may yield 19th-century domestic items and river travel artifacts.

name: Fillmore, Douglas County, IL
description: Fillmore in Douglas County was a small Illinois farming community platted in the 1840s–1850s, likely named in honor of President Millard Fillmore — a common political tribute naming convention of the era. The community served surrounding prairie farm families with a church, school, and store during the height of mid-19th century Illinois settlement before losing out commercially to railroad towns that bypassed the original plat. Today the Douglas County community of Fillmore has faded almost entirely, leaving only its entry in old plat books and county histories. Old church sites and road intersections at the historic plat location may yield antebellum American coins and mid-19th-century domestic artifacts.

name: Whistleville, IL
description: Whistleville was an Illinois community with an evocative name that likely derived either from the whistling of wind through a particular gap or hollow, the sound of a nearby steam mill or gin, or the nickname of a prominent local character. The community served as a minor rural center for surrounding Illinois farming families in the mid-to-late 19th century, with the usual church-and-store combination that anchored most frontier agricultural settlements. As surrounding townships consolidated and railroad-served towns captured the regional trade, Whistleville faded quietly. Old farmstead areas and road traces near the historic Whistleville community location may yield 19th-century coins, farm hardware, and domestic artifacts.

name: Midway, Fulton County, IL
description: Midway in Fulton County was a small Illinois community occupying a geographic midpoint along a road or trail through the agricultural country of west-central Illinois, one of several Illinois communities sharing this generic name. The community served surrounding farming families as a minor trade and social center during the mid-to-late 19th century, with a church and perhaps a country store. As improved transportation made geographic midpoints less commercially significant, Midway faded from the landscape. The old community site, identified through historic Fulton County plat records, may yield 19th-century domestic artifacts, early American coins, and agricultural hardware.

name: Fremont, IL
description: Fremont was a small Illinois community platted during the mid-19th century, likely named in honor of the famous Western explorer and presidential candidate John C. Frémont — a popular tribute name across the Midwest during the 1840s–1850s when Frémont was a national celebrity. The community served surrounding agricultural interests with a church and store before being eclipsed by railroad-served competitors. Today the community name is a historical footnote in Illinois settlement records. Old road intersections and farmstead perimeters near the historic Fremont community location may yield mid-19th-century coins, domestic items, and farm hardware for detectorists with landowner permission.

name: Griffin, IL
description: Griffin was a small Illinois farming community established during the mid-19th century settlement of the state, named for a prominent settler family that put down roots in the rich agricultural landscape of central or southern Illinois. The community served surrounding farming households with a church and country store, functioning as a minor social and commercial center before being bypassed by the economic consolidation of the late 19th century. Today Griffin survives only in old plat records, with no physical structures remaining above ground. Old farmstead locations and road traces near the historic Griffin community can yield 19th-century domestic artifacts, agricultural implements, and early American coins for detectorists with landowner permission.

name: Griggsville Landing, IL
description: Griggsville Landing was an Illinois river landing community on the Illinois River in Pike County, serving as a shipping point for the agricultural products of surrounding farms during the mid-19th century when river transport dominated commerce in the region. The community had warehouses, a tavern, and boat facilities at its height during the steamboat era of the 1840s–1860s, but declined steadily as the railroad expanded through Pike County and river commerce gave way to overland freight. Today the Illinois River landing is accessible as a recreation area, though the original commercial infrastructure is long gone. The riverbank area near the old landing may yield steamboat-era hardware, anchor chain fragments, and domestic items from the active trading era.

name: Milton, Brown County, IL
description: Milton in Brown County was a small Illinois river town on the Illinois River in Brown County, platted in the early settlement era of the 1820s–1840s when river commerce offered the most efficient route to market for the surrounding agricultural region. The town had a moderate commercial life during the peak steamboat era before the railroad made river landings less critical, and its population drained to better-connected neighboring communities through the latter half of the 19th century. A small historic cemetery may be the only surviving physical evidence of the Milton community today. The old townsite and river landing area are accessible for research and careful exploration, with antebellum domestic items and river hardware among the likely finds.

name: Sylvan, IL
description: Sylvan was an Illinois community platted during the optimistic settlement era of the mid-19th century, its name chosen to evoke the forested landscape of the Wisconsin-Illinois border country — a deliberate contrast to the treeless prairie that dominated much of the state. The community served surrounding farming families as a minor rural center with a church and small store, but never grew beyond a hamlet-scale settlement before being absorbed into the surrounding township structure. Today Sylvan exists only in old county records. Old church sites and farmstead perimeters near the historic Sylvan community location may yield mid-19th-century coins, domestic ceramics, and agricultural hardware.

name: Gurney, IL
description: Gurney was a small Illinois farming community established during the 19th century and named for a local settler family or an early landowner who helped develop the surrounding agricultural landscape. The community served as a minor crossroads trading center for surrounding farm families, with a church, school, and possibly a blacksmith shop at its height. As rural populations consolidated through the late 19th and early 20th centuries, Gurney faded from commercial significance and eventually from living memory. The historic Gurney community location, identified through old plat maps, may preserve 19th-century domestic artifacts, coins, and agricultural hardware for patient detectorists.

name: Shasta, IL
description: Shasta was a small Illinois farming community, one of many Illinois settlements that borrowed the name of the famous California peak in the wave of westward excitement following the 1848 Gold Rush when 'Shasta' had connotations of adventure and enterprise. The community served as a minor local center for surrounding agricultural families during the mid-to-late 19th century before being outcompeted by railroad-served towns. No physical evidence of the community survives above ground today. Old road intersections and farmstead sites near the historic Shasta community location can yield mid-19th-century coins, domestic fragments, and farm hardware.

name: Lexington, Edwards County, IL
description: Lexington in Edwards County was a small Illinois farming community in the southeastern corner of the state, established by early settlers in the rich agricultural country north of the Ohio River during the first decades of the 19th century. The community briefly served as a local trade center for surrounding farms growing corn and wheat, with a church and store supporting the community's social life, before larger Edwards County communities with better road connections absorbed its trade. Today the community exists only in county historical records, with no surviving structures. Searches around old church sites and farmstead perimeters in Edwards County near the Lexington location may yield early 19th-century coins and domestic artifacts.

name: Mills Prairie, IL
description: Mills Prairie was a small Illinois farming community in Edwards County, settled in the early 19th century on the open prairie grasslands that characterized the rolling terrain of Illinois's southeastern interior. The settlement drew its name from the prairie landscape itself — open, productive land that early settlers quickly converted to corn and wheat agriculture. The community had a church and a small store serving the surrounding homestead farms before being eclipsed by the growth of nearby Albion and other Edwards County centers. Today Mills Prairie is a historical placename in old county records. The old farmstead sites of Mills Prairie can yield early Illinois settlement-era artifacts, coins from the 1820s–1850s, and farm implements.

name: Palmyra, Edwards County, IL
description: Palmyra in Edwards County was a small Illinois community established in the early 19th century in the southeastern part of the state, one of many towns bearing the name of the ancient Syrian city — a popular naming choice among educated American settlers of the era who admired classical antiquity. The community served as a minor trade center for surrounding farms in Edwards County before being overshadowed by neighboring Albion, the county seat, which captured the regional commerce. Today Palmyra survives only in historical records and old county maps. The old community site, searched with landowner permission, may yield early 19th-century coins, domestic ceramics, and farm implements from the settlement era.

name: Piankashawtown, IL
description: Piankashawtown was a historic French and Native American settlement in Illinois, occupying a site in the Wabash River valley associated with the Piankashaw people — a Miami-language-speaking group related to the Miami and Wea nations — who lived in this region prior to and during the French colonial period of the 17th and 18th centuries. The site represents the intersection of Native American, French colonial, and early American settlement histories in what became southwestern Illinois, and was visited by French missionaries and traders before being displaced by American settlement after the 1820s. Today the historic location is of significant archaeological interest, and the site falls within areas managed for cultural resource protection. Systematic metal detecting is not recommended without proper permits, but the area holds historical significance for understanding the pre-American occupation of southern Illinois.

name: Wanborough, IL
description: Wanborough was an early Illinois community in Edwards County with strong English heritage, established by settlers from Wanborough, Surrey, England who emigrated to Illinois in the early 19th century as part of the English Prairie settlement movement organized by land agent George Flower and Morris Birkbeck. The community was part of the remarkable 1818–1820s English Prairie experiment in which educated English immigrants attempted to create an idyllic agrarian settlement in the American wilderness, with more genteel ambitions than typical frontier settlements. Wanborough's English character faded as American settlers outnumbered the original English colonists, and the community eventually dissolved into the surrounding Edwards County agricultural landscape. Artifacts from this early English settlement period — including English-made ceramics, coins, and domestic hardware — would be historically significant finds in the area.

name: Winchestertonfieldville, IA
description: Winchestertonfieldville was a tiny rural settlement in Iowa with an extraordinarily long name, made famous in the 2012 film 'The Words' and appearing in various lists of place name curiosities; the actual settlement it referred to was a minor farming community crossroads in Fayette County. The real community, known locally by simpler variants, was one of dozens of small Iowa farm villages that served surrounding homesteaders in the post–Civil War settlement era before improved transportation drew commerce to larger centers like West Union. Today the community area is productive Iowa farmland with no surviving commercial structures. Old farmstead sites and road traces near the historic community location can yield late 19th-century Iowa farm artifacts and coins.

name: Lakewood, IA
description: Lakewood was a small Iowa farming community, established in the post–Civil War settlement era when Iowa's remaining prairie lands were rapidly converted to agriculture by families pushing westward from Ohio, Indiana, and Illinois. The community served as a minor local center for surrounding farms with a church, school, and country store before rural depopulation through the early 20th century drew its residents to larger Iowa centers. Today the community exists only in old Iowa plat maps. The surrounding Iowa farmland, worked with landowner permission, may yield late 19th-century domestic artifacts, farm implements, and coins from the active community era.

name: Big Spring, IA
description: Big Spring was an Iowa community built around a reliable natural spring that provided water for livestock, travelers, and settlers during the crucial first decades of Euro-American settlement when reliable water sources were the primary determinant of community location. The spring served as a gathering point that attracted a tavern, a store, and eventually a church and school, but as drilled wells became available and the spring's geographic advantage faded, so did the community's commercial significance. Today the original spring site is likely on private farmland, possibly still flowing or now buried by agricultural modifications. The old spring area and surrounding farmstead sites can yield 19th-century domestic artifacts and travel-era hardware.

name: Iowaville, IA
description: Iowaville was a significant early Iowa settlement on the Des Moines River in Van Buren County, established in the 1830s as one of the first American communities in what would become Iowa, at the site of a former Sauk and Fox village near a Des Moines River ford. The community thrived briefly as a river trading post and regional center before being eclipsed by Keosauqua and other better-situated county towns, eventually losing its post office and commercial life by the 1850s. The Des Moines River bottomlands around the old Iowaville site are prone to flooding, which has both preserved and scattered the archaeological record. River terrace areas near the historic site, accessible on public land along the river, may yield early 19th-century trade items, Native American-contact-era artifacts, and pioneer domestic hardware.

name: Coal Valley, IA
description: Coal Valley was an Iowa mining community established in the mid-to-late 19th century to exploit the shallow coal deposits that run through much of southern Iowa's Pennsylvanian-age geology, providing fuel for homes, railroads, and small industries across the region during an era when coal was the primary energy source. The community had a company store, a school, and housing for miners and their families at its height, functioning as a self-contained mining camp. As deeper and more productive Illinois and West Virginia coal undersold Iowa's shallow deposits after World War I, most Iowa coal camps closed by the 1920s–1930s, leaving behind only shaft collars, slag heaps, and deteriorating company housing. The old mine dump areas and former company town footprint can yield industrial hardware, mining company tokens, and early 20th-century domestic artifacts.

name: Conover, IA
description: Conover was a small Iowa farming community established during the rapid agricultural settlement of the state in the 1850s–1870s, serving surrounding homesteaders with a church, school, and country store. The community was named for an early settler family or local landowner and functioned as a minor rural center before improved transportation and rural consolidation drew its residents toward larger nearby Iowa towns. Today Conover exists only in old county plat records. The surrounding Iowa farmland, explored with landowner permission, may yield late 19th-century coins, farm implements, and domestic artifacts from the community's active decades.

name: Littleport, IA
description: Littleport was a small Iowa community in Clayton County on the Upper Iowa River, established in the mid-19th century as a minor river landing and farming center that served the dairy and agricultural country of northeastern Iowa. The community had a brief commercial life serving local farmers and rivermen before the railroads bypassed it and the trade shifted to larger Clayton County centers. Today Littleport is a small unincorporated community with a handful of residents, though most of its 19th-century commercial infrastructure has long disappeared. The old river landing area and surrounding farm sites may yield 19th-century domestic artifacts and river trade hardware.

name: Motor, IA
description: Motor was a small Iowa farming community in Adair County, established in the late 19th century and likely named for its position along an early road or the optimistic spirit of the new automotive age. The community had a post office for a period and served surrounding farm families as a minor rural center before the consolidation of rural services drew commerce toward larger Adair County towns. Today Motor is a historical name in Iowa records with no surviving commercial structures. Old farmstead sites and road traces near the historic Motor community location can yield late 19th-century and early 20th-century Iowa farm artifacts and domestic items.

name: Donnan, IA
description: Donnan was a small Iowa farming community in Howard County, established in the post–Civil War settlement era by farm families seeking the rich agricultural land of northeastern Iowa's rolling hills and prairie. The community served surrounding farms with a church, school, and country store, functioning as a minor local center before rural depopulation through the 20th century emptied it. Today the Donnan area is active Iowa farmland with no surviving commercial structures. Old farmstead perimeters and road traces near the historic Donnan community location may yield late 19th-century coins, farm hardware, and domestic artifacts.

name: Siegel, IA
description: Siegel was a small Iowa farming community, established during the late 19th century settlement of the state's agricultural interior and likely named for a German immigrant family — the Siegel surname being common among German-speaking settlers who flooded into Iowa, Missouri, and Illinois after 1848. The community served surrounding German-American farm families with a church, school, and country store, functioning as a minor ethnic enclave and rural center. As the German-American population assimilated and rural communities consolidated through the 20th century, Siegel faded from commercial significance. Old German-American church sites and farmstead perimeters near the historic Siegel community location may yield late 19th-century coins, domestic hardware, and German-American cultural artifacts.

name: Doris, IA
description: Doris was a small Iowa farming community established in the late 19th or early 20th century, likely named for the daughter or wife of an early landowner or railroad official — a common naming convention for small Midwestern communities platted alongside newly constructed rail lines. The community served surrounding farm families as a minor rural center with a grain elevator, a store, and a church, before rural consolidation and declining farm populations emptied it through the mid-20th century. Today the community site is active Iowa farmland. Old elevator foundations and farmstead perimeters near the historic Doris community location may yield early 20th-century coins, grain handling hardware, and domestic items.

name: Hughes, IA
description: Hughes was a small Iowa farming community in the late 19th century, likely named for a prominent landowner or early settler family that held significant acreage in the surrounding township. The community served as a minor local center for surrounding farm families with a church and store, functioning through the post–Civil War agricultural boom before rural depopulation drew its residents toward larger Iowa centers. Today Hughes exists only in old county plat records. Farm field margins and road traces near the historic Hughes community location in Iowa may yield late 19th-century domestic artifacts, farm implements, and early American coins.

name: Dudley, IA
description: Dudley was a small Iowa farming community established during the rapid agricultural settlement of the state in the 1860s–1880s, serving surrounding homesteaders with a church, school, and country store typical of the small service communities that anchored Iowa's rural townships. The community was named for an early settler family or local landowner and functioned as a minor rural center before improved roads and automobiles allowed Iowa farm families to travel easily to larger nearby towns for goods and services. Today Dudley is an inactive community with no surviving commercial structures. Old farmstead sites and road traces near the historic Dudley location may yield late 19th-century Iowa farm artifacts, coins, and domestic hardware.

name: Plum Creek, IA
description: Plum Creek was an Iowa community that grew up along a creek lined with native wild plum thickets — a common landscape feature on the Iowa prairie that provided fruit for early settlers and gave dozens of Iowa creeks and communities their name. The settlement functioned as a minor rural center for surrounding farming families in the mid-to-late 19th century, with a church and store anchoring the community's social life. As the wild plum thickets gave way to cultivated fields and the creek was ditched for agricultural drainage, the community's identity faded along with the landscape feature that named it. Old farmstead sites and creek-margin areas near the historic Plum Creek community may yield 19th-century domestic artifacts and farm hardware.

name: Thrall, IA
description: Thrall was a small Iowa farming community established during the agricultural settlement era of the 1870s–1880s, named for a local landowner or early settler family who platted the community along a country road or creek in the rolling Iowa countryside. The community had a church, school, and country store at its height, serving surrounding farm families before rural consolidation and improved transportation drew the commerce to larger nearby Iowa towns. Today Thrall is absorbed into the surrounding agricultural landscape with no surviving commercial structures. Old church sites and road traces near the historic Thrall community location can yield late 19th-century coins, domestic artifacts, and farm implements.

name: Secor, IA
description: Secor was an Iowa community established in the late 19th century, possibly related to a rail line or named for a local landowner, serving surrounding agricultural interests in one of Iowa's many small rural townships during the post–Civil War settlement era. The community had a brief commercial life centered on agriculture-related services before the rural Iowa population began consolidating in larger centers through the early 20th century. Today Secor is an inactive community with no surviving commercial infrastructure. Old farmstead perimeters and road traces near the historic Secor community location may yield late 19th-century to early 20th-century Iowa farm artifacts and domestic items.

name: Rubens, IA
description: Rubens was a small Iowa farming community in the late 19th century, named for a local landowner or perhaps reflecting the cultural aspirations of educated European immigrant settlers who admired the Flemish painter Peter Paul Rubens. The community served surrounding farm families as a minor rural center before being absorbed into the larger township structure as Iowa's rural population consolidated. Today the community exists only in old Iowa county records and plat maps. Old farmstead sites and road traces near the historic Rubens community location may yield late 19th-century domestic artifacts, European-American cultural items, and Iowa farm hardware.

name: Herring, IA
description: Herring was a small Iowa farming community, likely named for an early settler family, established during the rapid agricultural settlement of Iowa's interior in the 1870s–1890s. The community served surrounding farm families with a church and country store, functioning as a minor rural gathering point before improved transportation drew commerce away to larger centers. Today the community name survives only in old Iowa county records. Farm field margins and old road traces near the historic Herring community location can yield late 19th-century coins, domestic items, and agricultural hardware for detectorists with landowner permission.

name: Preparation, IA
description: Preparation was a unique Iowa community in Monona County established in the 1850s by a utopian religious community led by Charles Etienne Remy, a French religious leader who gathered followers seeking to prepare for the Second Coming of Christ in a communal farming settlement on the bluffs above the Missouri River. The community was organized along communal principles with shared property and religious governance, growing to several hundred members before internal conflicts and Remy's death dissolved the experiment by the late 1850s. Today the Preparation townsite area on the Missouri River bluffs is part of the Iowa landscape near Castana, with some archaeological evidence of the communal settlement surviving. The unusual nature of the community — a French-led utopian colony on the Iowa frontier — makes it a historically significant target for researchers and artifact hunters with proper landowner permissions.

name: Mount Pisgah, IA
description: Mount Pisgah was a significant Mormon pioneer settlement in Montgomery County, Iowa, established in 1846 as one of the major way stations on the Mormon Trail as Latter-day Saint refugees fled Nauvoo, Illinois following the assassination of Joseph Smith and the destruction of their community. At its peak the temporary settlement housed thousands of Saints who farmed the bottomlands, built shelters, and rested before continuing the trek to the Great Salt Lake Valley, with many dying there from exposure, disease, and starvation in the harsh Iowa winters. Today a historic Mormon marker and small park preserve the memory of Mount Pisgah, and the surrounding area is private Iowa farmland. The dense layering of pioneer occupation makes this a historically significant site, though the sacred nature of the ground warrants respectful exploration; surrounding fields have yielded pioneer hardware, wagon parts, and mid-19th-century domestic items.

name: Unique, IA
description: Unique was a small Iowa farming community with an appropriately distinctive name, established during the late 19th century agricultural settlement of the state and serving surrounding farm families as a minor rural center with a church and country store. Despite its singular name, Unique followed the typical arc of small Iowa farm towns — brief commercial viability followed by rural depopulation through the early 20th century as automobiles allowed farmers to reach larger service centers. Today Unique exists only in Iowa state historical records. Old church sites and road traces near the historic Unique community location can yield late 19th-century Iowa farm artifacts and domestic items.

name: Morrisburgh, IA
description: Morrisburgh was a small Iowa farming community established during the late 19th century settlement era, likely named for an early settler or land developer of Irish origin — the Morriss or Morris surname being common among Irish-American settlers who moved into Iowa following the Great Famine migration of the late 1840s. The community served surrounding farm families as a minor rural center before rural depopulation through the 20th century dissolved it. Today the community exists only in historical records. Old farmstead perimeters and road traces near the historic Morrisburgh community location may yield late 19th-century coins, domestic hardware, and Irish-American cultural artifacts.

name: Ivanhoe, IA
description: Ivanhoe was an Iowa farming community platted during the mid-to-late 19th century, bearing the name of Sir Walter Scott's famous 1819 novel — a popular literary reference among educated American settlers who gave their communities aspirational names from popular culture and classical sources. The community served surrounding agricultural interests with a church and store before being eclipsed by better-connected neighboring Iowa towns. Today Ivanhoe is a historical name in Iowa county records. Old church sites and road intersections near the historic Ivanhoe community location in Iowa may yield 19th-century domestic artifacts and early American coins.

name: Hinkletown, IA
description: Hinkletown was a small Iowa farming community, likely named for the Hinkle or Hinkley family — Germanic surnames common among Pennsylvania Dutch settlers who spread from Ohio and Indiana into Iowa in the mid-19th century. The community served surrounding farm families as a minor rural gathering point with a church and store during the post–Civil War agricultural boom before rural consolidation through the early 20th century emptied it. Today Hinkletown exists only in old Iowa county records. Old farmstead sites and road traces near the historic Hinkletown community location can yield late 19th-century coins, Pennsylvania Dutch cultural artifacts, and farm implements.

name: Hopewell, IA
description: Hopewell in Iowa was a small farming community, bearing one of the most common place names in the Midwest — a name that reflected the aspirations of religious settlers, often Methodists, Baptists, or Presbyterians, who built churches and communities based on the biblical connotations of hope and divine blessing. The community served surrounding farm families as a minor rural center in the post–Civil War settlement era before rural depopulation through the 20th century dissolved it into the surrounding Iowa agricultural landscape. Today the community name survives only in historical records. Old church sites and farmstead perimeters near the historic Iowa Hopewell community location can yield 19th-century religious community artifacts, domestic items, and farm hardware.

name: Midway, Johnson County, IA
description: Midway in Johnson County was a small Iowa community in the rural agricultural landscape between Iowa City and other Johnson County centers, occupying a geographic midpoint position along an early road that served surrounding farm families with a church and store in the mid-to-late 19th century. The community never developed beyond a hamlet scale before improved transportation drew the commerce to Iowa City and other larger centers. Today the Midway area is active Johnson County farmland surrounding the greater Iowa City metropolitan area. Old farmstead sites and road traces in the historic Midway location near Iowa City may yield 19th-century coins, domestic artifacts, and farm implements.

name: Riceville, ME
description: Riceville in Maine was a small rural community in Oxford or Franklin County, established during the 18th or early 19th century by farming families seeking agricultural land in Maine's interior hill country above the coastal lowlands. The community likely had a sawmill and a gristmill powered by a local stream, along with a church and school serving the surrounding farmsteads, before Maine's harsh winters, thin soils, and economic pull of coastal cities gradually drained its population through the 19th century. Today the site is forested private land in Maine's interior with no surviving commercial structures. Old mill pond sites and farmstead cellar holes near the historic Riceville community can yield 18th-to-19th-century New England domestic artifacts and farm implements.

name: Perkins, ME
description: Perkins was a small Maine community established by farming families in the early 19th century on the forested uplands of Maine's interior, one of dozens of small hill towns platted during the period of optimistic inland settlement when Revolutionary War veterans and their families pushed into Maine's backcountry seeking land grants and agricultural opportunity. Thin, rocky soils and brutal winters made farming difficult, and as coastal and urban Maine offered better economic opportunities through the mid-19th century, Perkins slowly depopulated. Today the community is unincorporated or merged with a neighboring town, with old stone walls, cellar holes, and a cemetery as the primary physical evidence. New England farmstead cellar holes are productive sites for 19th-century domestic ceramics, coins, and hand-forged iron implements.

name: Lucin, UT
description: Lucin was a remote railroad community in Box Elder County, Utah, situated on the salt flats of the Great Basin where the Southern Pacific Railroad's Lucin Cutoff — built in 1903 across the Great Salt Lake on a wooden trestle — had its western terminus. The town served as a maintenance hub for the remarkable wooden trestle railroad crossing that reduced the transcontinental rail distance by 44 miles, housing railroad workers and their families in the isolated desert. When the wooden trestle was replaced by a rock fill causeway in 1959, the need for the Lucin maintenance facility vanished, and the town was quickly abandoned. The desert-preserved site retains some structural remains and mine tailings from associated mineral exploration, and the BLM land surrounding the old townsite is open to exploration for railroad hardware, worker camp artifacts, and early 20th-century coins.

name: Katalla, AK
description: Katalla was a brief but extraordinary boomtown in southcentral Alaska, situated near the Bering River coalfields and a promising natural oil seep on the Gulf of Alaska coast, which in 1902–1907 attracted speculators and entrepreneurs hoping to make it the major port and industrial center for Alaska's vast interior mineral wealth. The town boasted hotels, saloons, a newspaper, and grand plans for a railroad to the Copper River country before the competing Cordova railroad and port prevailed, and a devastating harbor storm in 1907 destroyed the breakwater and dashed Katalla's commercial hopes. An oil refinery operated there until 1933, making it the first commercial oil operation in Alaska, but the town subsequently emptied completely. Today Katalla is a remote, overgrown site on the Gulf of Alaska coast accessible only by small plane or boat, where building remains and industrial hardware survive in the wet boreal rainforest.

name: Redwood, AK
description: Redwood was a small community in Alaska, likely a minor settlement associated with the early 20th-century resource extraction economy — logging, fishing, or mining — that characterized remote Alaskan communities of the territorial era. Like many Alaska bush communities, Redwood was dependent on a single industry or a government function such as a cannery, a trading post, or a roadhouse, and when that anchor disappeared, so did the community. Today the site is likely forested or coastal land in Alaska's vast wilderness, accessible only by small aircraft or water. The isolation of most former Alaskan communities makes organized artifact hunting impractical, though historically significant items from the territorial era may survive at the site.

name: Fullersburg, IL
description: Fullersburg was a small community in DuPage County, Illinois, established as a milling and manufacturing village along Salt Creek in the 1830s–1850s by early settlers who harnessed the creek's water power for sawing timber and grinding grain for the surrounding agricultural region.
