The community had a hotel, a post office, and several mills at its height, serving as one of the more commercially active settlements in early DuPage County before the railroads and the growing city of Chicago absorbed the regional commerce. Today the Fullersburg Woods area along Salt Creek is a forest preserve managed by the DuPage County Forest Preserve District, with a nature center and maintained trails. The preserve's protected status means metal detecting is not permitted within its boundaries, but the rich early Illinois settlement history of the area makes it a fascinating site to visit.

name: Keystone, KS
description: Keystone was a small Kansas farming community established during the great late 19th-century settlement of the Kansas plains, named in the tradition of communities that evoked strength and centrality — the keystone being the central architectural element that locks an arch together. The community served surrounding wheat farmers and cattlemen with a church, grain elevator, and country store during the height of the Kansas agricultural boom before the combination of repeated drought, low commodity prices, and rural depopulation through the mid-20th century emptied it. Today the site is Kansas agricultural land with perhaps a grain elevator foundation or church remnant. Old elevator sites and farmstead perimeters in the Keystone area may yield late 19th-century to early 20th-century coins, farm hardware, and domestic items.

name: Clarenceville, MI
description: Clarenceville was a small Michigan community in the southeastern part of the state — now largely absorbed into the greater Detroit metropolitan area — established during the mid-19th century farming era when the rich agricultural land of Wayne County supported dozens of small market communities serving local farm families. The town served as a minor rural trading center before being engulfed by suburban expansion spreading west from Detroit through the 20th century. Today the Clarenceville area is suburban Livonia or Redford Township, with its 19th-century agricultural past buried under residential and commercial development. Original farmstead sites occasionally surface during construction, sometimes revealing early American coins, farm implements, and domestic hardware from the pre-suburban era.

name: Glastenbury, VT
description: Glastenbury was a Vermont township in Bennington County that was organized in 1834 but never successfully settled, with the forested mountain terrain of the Green Mountains proving too inhospitable for sustained farming — a pattern common in Vermont's remote hill towns where thin soils, brutal winters, and isolated terrain defeated repeated settlement attempts. A small charcoal-making and logging community briefly occupied the town in the mid-to-late 19th century, supporting a handful of families who supplied charcoal to nearby iron furnaces, but the population dropped to single digits by the early 20th century and Glastenbury became a 'ghost town' township. The area is now a designated wilderness within Green Mountain National Forest, and the remote forest terrain holds old charcoal mounds, logging-era debris, and stone foundations visible to hikers. Metal detecting is prohibited within National Forest Wilderness designations, but the area attracts hikers interested in Vermont's vanished hill town history.

name: Lewiston, VT
description: Lewiston was a Vermont hill town in Windham or Orange County that experienced the typical cycle of early 19th-century settlement optimism followed by rural abandonment as Vermont's rocky upland farms proved unsustainable against the competition of richer Midwestern agricultural land. Families who had carved farms from Vermont's forested hills in the 1800s–1840s abandoned them by the 1870s–1900s as young people emigrated to urban factories and western farms. Today the former Lewiston township is forested land with stone walls, cellar holes, and a historic cemetery as the surviving evidence of its settlement era. Vermont cellar hole sites are productive for New England domestic ceramics, hand-forged iron implements, and 19th-century coins — though most are on private or state forest land requiring permission or permit.

name: Ricker Basin, VT
description: Ricker Basin was a Vermont hill farming settlement in the Green Mountains of Washington County, occupied by the Ricker family and a small cluster of neighboring farms during the early 19th century before the steep mountain terrain, thin soils, and economic pressures of mid-century rural Vermont drove the last families out by the 1880s. The Ricker family homestead site and an associated cemetery survive in what is now the Groton State Forest, accessible by hiking trail from the Kettle Pond area. Stone foundation ruins, old stone walls, and orchard remnants mark the former farmstead locations, creating an evocative landscape that is popular with hikers and history enthusiasts. Metal detecting in Vermont state forests requires a permit, but the site is an exceptional example of Vermont's extensive ghost farm heritage.

name: Beebe River, NH
description: Beebe River was a New Hampshire lumber and mill community in Carroll County, established in the 19th century around a series of water-powered sawmills and later a clapboard mill that processed the vast timber resources of the White Mountain foothills. The town grew to support mill workers with a company store, housing, and community buildings during the height of the White Mountain timber era before the mills closed and the population dispersed in the early 20th century. Today the Beebe River area retains some structural remains from the mill era, situated on private land or within state forests of the White Mountains region. The mill pond earthworks and surrounding worker community sites may preserve early 20th-century industrial hardware, company-store tokens, and domestic artifacts.

name: Kilkenny, NH
description: Kilkenny was a New Hampshire township in Coos County — the state's wild and remote northern tip — that was granted in 1774 but never developed into a functioning community, with its dense spruce and fir forest, wet terrain, and harsh climate defeating every attempt at sustained agricultural settlement. The township today is part of the White Mountain National Forest and is crossed by the Kilkenny Ridge Trail, making it a destination for hikers rather than settlers. The occasional 19th-century logging camp or trapper's cabin site may survive in the deep forest. The national forest designation prohibits metal detecting, but the landscape preserves an authentic view of what the North Country looked like before Euro-American settlement changed it.

name: Livermore, NH
description: Livermore was a New Hampshire hill town in Grafton County, originally granted in 1876 and briefly occupied by a small logging and lumber community that harvested the vast spruce and fir forests of the Pemigewasset Wilderness during the late 19th century. The Livermore Lumber Company operated a sawmill and maintained worker housing, a railroad spur, and company facilities in the wilderness interior for several decades before the timber was exhausted and operations ceased by the early 20th century. Today the former township of Livermore is within the White Mountain National Forest, and the old railroad grades are hiking trails through the Pemi Wilderness. The national forest wilderness designation prohibits metal detecting, but the logging-era infrastructure remnants — old logging camp sites, rail spikes, and mill pond earthworks — are visible to hikers.

name: Monson, NH
description: Monson was a New Hampshire hill town in Hillsborough County that was granted in 1748 but achieved only limited settlement before the population dispersed during the 19th century as New Hampshire's upland farms proved economically unsustainable. The rocky terrain and thin soils that discouraged sustained agriculture left behind a landscape of stone walls, cellar holes, and old orchards that mark the locations of former farmsteads. Today the former Monson township is part of Greenfield State Park or adjacent private land in Hillsborough County. Stone-wall-enclosed old fields and cellar hole sites are the most productive areas for New England ghost town detectorists, who may find 18th-to-19th-century coins, domestic ceramics, and hand-forged farm implements.

name: Bartlett, ND
description: Bartlett was a small North Dakota farming community on the northern Great Plains, established during the great homestead rush of the 1890s–1910s when the federal government opened Dakota lands to settlement and thousands of families filed on 160-acre claims across the region's wheat-growing prairie. The community had a grain elevator, a general store, and a church serving surrounding homesteaders during the wheat-growing boom years before the combination of drought, low wheat prices, and the Great Depression collapsed the North Dakota homestead economy in the 1930s. Today the Bartlett area is active wheat farmland with perhaps a deteriorating elevator or church foundation as the only reminder of the community. The surrounding flat agricultural land, worked with landowner permission, may yield early 20th-century coins, homestead hardware, and farm implements.

name: Gold Point, NV
description: Gold Point was a silver and gold mining camp in Esmeralda County, Nevada, known variously as Hornsilver and Lime Point before settling on its current name, booming in the 1860s and again briefly in the early 20th century as repeated silver discoveries drew optimistic miners to the remote desert range between Goldfield and Beatty. The town had a post office, hotel, saloons, and a peak population of several hundred during its most active periods before ore depletion and the collapse of Nevada's silver economy reduced it to a handful of die-hard residents. Gold Point is today one of Nevada's most accessible ghost towns — a small but authentic collection of deteriorating adobe and wood-frame buildings still standing in the high desert — maintained by a private owner who welcomes visitors. The surrounding desert mine dumps and old claim areas are rich territory for period mining tools, ore specimens, and desert-preserved camp artifacts.

name: Nevadaville, CO
description: Nevadaville was a significant gold mining camp in Gilpin County, Colorado, established in 1859 immediately after the Pike's Peak gold rush discoveries and growing alongside Central City as one of the most productive gold camps in the territory, with lode gold deposits yielding millions in ore over the following decades. The camp had over 3,000 residents at its peak, with multiple quartz mills, a stamp mill, saloons, churches, and a school serving the hard-rock mining community until declining gold prices and ore depletion reduced the population through the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Today Nevadaville's remaining structures sit on the hillside above Central City, and portions of the old townsite are accessible via roads from the Central City gaming district. The mine dumps and old claim areas surrounding Nevadaville are rich territory for mining artifacts, miner's checks, and period tools — though access to some areas may require coordination with local mine claim holders.

name: Dearfield, CO
description: Dearfield was a pioneering African American agricultural colony in Weld County, Colorado, founded in 1910 by O.T. Jackson and other Black entrepreneurs as part of a Back-to-the-Land movement that sought to create self-sufficient Black farming communities on the Colorado plains during an era of intense racial segregation. At its peak in the early 1920s, Dearfield had nearly 700 residents, farms, a post office, a church, a grocery store, and a dance hall — an extraordinary achievement given the racial and economic barriers facing Black settlers on the high plains. The Great Depression, drought, and racial economic discrimination combined to empty the colony by the 1940s, and most structures were demolished or collapsed. Today the Dearfield site in Weld County retains a few deteriorating structures, is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, and is the subject of ongoing preservation efforts — a powerful symbol of African American pioneering on the Colorado frontier.

name: Adelaide, CO
description: Adelaide in Gunnison County was a small Colorado coal mining camp that served the coal-bearing formations of the Elk Mountain area during the late 19th and early 20th centuries, housing miners who worked the underground seams that fueled regional industries and railroad operations. The camp had company housing, a store, and basic community facilities before the mines were exhausted or became uneconomical and the camp was shuttered. Today the site is private land in Gunnison County's mountain terrain, with mine shaft collars and tailings piles marking the locations of former underground workings. Coal mining camp artifacts — brass lamp parts, mining tokens, company store merchandise — are the typical finds at such sites for detectorists with landowner access.

name: Adelaide, Lake County, CO
description: Adelaide in Lake County was a small Colorado mining camp in the high terrain near Leadville, established during the silver mining boom of the 1870s–1880s when the Leadville district became one of the richest silver fields in the American West, attracting tens of thousands of miners and supporting dozens of satellite camps across the surrounding mountains. The camp served as a minor service point and housing cluster for miners working nearby claims, lacking the full commercial infrastructure of larger Leadville-district towns. Today the Lake County site is on or near the public lands managed by the U.S. Forest Service in the San Isabel National Forest area. The high-altitude mine dumps and old camp sites of the Leadville district are among the most productive in Colorado for mining-era artifacts — with miner's tools, ore samples, and period hardware common finds.

name: Arapahoe, CO
description: Arapahoe was a small Colorado community, possibly a stagecoach stop or minor railroad watering point serving travelers crossing the eastern Colorado plains during the 1870s–1890s when the cattle and wheat economy of the high plains was just developing. The community served a limited role for surrounding homesteaders and cattle ranchers before better road connections and later the automobile made such minor waypoints economically obsolete. Today the Arapahoe area of Colorado is agricultural plains with no surviving commercial infrastructure from the original community. The high plains around the historic Arapahoe community location, searched with landowner permission, can yield late 19th-century coins, homestead hardware, and cattle-drive era artifacts.

name: Arrow, CO
description: Arrow was a small Colorado mining or ranching community, established during the late 19th century in one of Colorado's many mountain or foothill valleys where mineral prospecting, ranching, or railroad construction created brief clusters of population. The community served as a minor local center before the resource that created it was exhausted or bypassed by other developments, leaving behind scattered structural ruins and mine workings. Today the Arrow community site is likely public or private land in Colorado's mountain terrain. Mine dump areas and old camp sites near the historic Arrow location can yield late 19th-century mining hardware, ranch implements, and domestic artifacts.
